summary: *Compañía presidial* (muster) — 1 capt, 1 tte, 1 cadet, 3 sgts, 5 cabos, 59 soldados = 70 men; invalids 1 sgt/2 sol. ⭐⭐ records that on 20 June [1799] two frigates anchored at Cabo San Lucas, their crews landing to take on water; the 21st the area sergeant-commander roused [his men]; though the ships fired nine howitzer rounds of grapeshot at him, the crews managed to re-embark, abandoning a water-store, and set sail at once

author: Antonio Grajera
summary: *Compañía presidial* (muster) — 1 tte, 2 alféreces, 1 sgt, 5 cabos, 51 soldados = 60 men; 6 gunners at the fort (1 cabo + 5); 25 Catalonian Volunteers (1 tte, 1 sgt, 3 cabos, 20 sol.); invalids 1 graduated tte, 3 sgts, 2 cabos, 5 sol.

author: José Joaquín de Arrillaga (Loreto); endorsed by Diego de Borica
themes: personnel; arrillaga; service-record; founding-generation; back-pay; reglamento; romeu; santa-catalina-martir; colorado-river; borica; governorship
summary: *Petición al Rey* (see expanded entry) — service record (22 yr 6 mo; 16 yr as Loreto captain); champions back-pay for the founding-generation veterans who discovered San Diego & Monterey; recounts liquidating the five presidios' accounts 1781–1792 (traversing all Baja Calif.); during his interim governorship a mission was founded; his summer-1796 land reconnaissance founded Mission Santa Catalina Mártir and he was attacked three times by the Colorado rancherías (killed only 5 'though he could have killed many more'); asks to be considered for the proprietary governorship should Borica's illness vacate it. Borica's signed marginal endorsement certifies all of it

author: Ignacio Vallejo

author: Ignacio Vallejo

author: Sgt Pedro Amador
themes: indian-raiding; military; amador; sf; santa-clara; san-jose; expedition; fugitives
summary: *Salida á contra Indios* — punitive expedition (8–15 May): from Santa Clara, found the relocated rancheria the 9th, Indians fled to the Sierra; used lance & sword, killed a captain, others fled; took 10 prisoners to the San José escort; on the 11th fell on 7 rancherías and recovered 21 Christian fugitives handed over by the captains (no gentile would convert); disarmed the gentiles (seized bows & arrows) as punishment 'for daring to bear arms against us'; identified men who had helped kill [SF] Christians; one captain, run through by a lance, kept throwing stones and grasped the lance with his hands; reached San Francisco the 15th

author: José [Darío] Argüello

author: Gov. Arrillaga
themes: personnel; aguilar; service-record; founding-generation; cabo-san-lucas; betsey-of-london; anglo-spanish-war; baja-california
summary: *Hoja de servicio del Sargento [Javier] Aguilar* — entered service 16 Oct 1768; 32 yr 3 mo total, always in the Loreto company; present at the exploration & founding of San Diego and Monterey and all expeditions there 1768–1771; many actions vs. gentiles (last 1785 in the San Diego sierra, 12 gentiles killed); entrusted with most *cimarrón* expeditions for 12 yr; ⭐ 24 June 1799 at Cabo San Lucas killed some of the English — though the two frigates fired nine cannon shots, they then withdrew; commands the southern district 5½ yr + the militia company 14 mo

author: Felipe de Goycoechea & escribano Ignacio Martínez

summary: *Ejecución de una sentencia* — soldier José Antonio Rosas (age 18, of the Pueblo de Los Ángeles) executed for bestiality (with a mule) committed 20 June 1800 at Mission San Buenaventura, seen by Indians; sentenced by the Auditor de Guerra to be hanged & his body burned with the mule — no hangman, so shot then burned; prosecuted by alférez Pablo de Cota (fiscal), defended by Sgt José Ortega; case sent to México; convicted & confessed (the original process fills these pages)

themes: justice; crime; bestiality; sumaria; san-buenaventura; indigenous-testimony; women; rosas; cota; duplicate
summary: full verbatim *sumaria* in the José Antonio Rosas bestiality case (see expanded entry). Process begun 23 June 1800; fiscal Pablo de Cota, escribano Ignacio Martínez, interpreter José Mª Domínguez (sworn, for the Indian witnesses); reported to Fr. Vicente de Santa María; ⭐⭐ depositions of two Indian neophyte women — María Ambrosia & María Tomasa; mule of vecino Agustín Leiva; Rosas's confession; Sgt José Mª Ortega's mercy defense (Rosas tried to bribe the witnesses; 'distinguished from the Indians only by his clothing'); fiscal & Auditor de Guerra death sentence, Viceroy's approval; executed by firing squad 11 Feb 1801, body burned with the mule. ⚠ Savage's note: 'not fully extracted when vol. XXVIII was gone through; taken from pp.487–533' — so this is the full copy of the Doc 222 original (same doc, two extractions). ✓ Confirms the Cota death (cert. says he was 'ya menos')

author: Comte. Luis Argüello (juez fiscal; escribano artillero Matías Guerra) · Tte. José Mª Estudillo (*defensor*)
summary: Causa criminal into a homicide by Mission San Francisco neophytes — Jacobo (principal author), Apolinario, and the blind Tenbis/Toribio (accomplices): Jacobo beat Ebacio[?] to death from jealousy over a woman he wished to marry. Estudillo's long *defensa*: the accused are 'recién convertidos,' rustic Indians; the missionary ministers themselves flog neophytes '12, 15 ó 25 azotes' for woman-theft/adultery/homicide; pleads not for acquittal but for exemplary *destierro de Provincias* instead of death. Outcome: convicted on confession; case routed Arrillaga→Viceroy→*auditor de guerra* twice (defect: no *curador* at the neophytes' ratification); Toribio died at Mission SF 28 Nov 1809, attended by Fray Ramón Abella; auditor (28 Sep 1810) praised Estudillo's defensa 'muy buena y útil'; sentence: 10 years' presidio at Monterey + afflictive lashes (Apolinario partly exempted); approved by Viceroy Venegas, 10 Oct 1810. Rich on mission discipline, neophyte legal status & indigenous custom.

summary: Causa criminal vs. various Angelenos for 'excesos del juego, bebida, y pendencias.' Embeds Sgt. Javier Alvarado's 21 June report: rampant drunkenness, the jail/guardhouse full, men named — Ignacio Valencia (jailed), José Antº Nieto, Capitán Varela, Manuel Moreno, Molina. Measures: suspend wine & aguardiente sales except from Mission San Gabriel; after 8 pm no riding through the pueblo unless working. Finding: Valencia resisted justice, struck alcalde Guillermo Soto, and wounded Capitán Varela with a spur — all from drink; fiscal asks the Gob. for an exemplary punishment. Rich pueblo social-history (alcohol, disorder, civil-military authority). Orig. pp.586–603

author: Comte. Luis Argüello
summary: 'Establecimiento ruso' — three starving Russian deserters from Kuskov's Fort Ross. A little flag was sighted on the high hill across the bay (5 Dec); the launch was sent and brought back three Russians found on the beach 'en la última miseria,' dying of hunger. They declared they had deserted 'del establecimiento ruso… más arriba de Bodega' (Fort Ross) for the great hunger there; that there was no ship but one beached; *'que su Comandante Coscof [Kuskov] trabaja de duro en fábrica; que ya tienen cuatro casas grandes; pero no saben… cuál será el motivo de establecerse allí.'* Primary account of Fort Ross's first months. Converges w/ C-A 12 (Kuskov/Eayrs), C-A 13 (RAC parlamentario), C-A 55 (Kuskov supply). Orig. pp.508–9

author: Tte. José Mª Estudillo
summary: Investigation into the death of P. Fr. Andrés Quintana (Mission Santa Cruz, morning of 12 Oct 1812). The *diligencias* (begun 14th, concluded 22nd) found *'no hay indicios de que hubiese violencia en la muerte repentina del Padre'* — declared a natural sudden death, the padre long gravely ill. ⚠ Historically this is the celebrated Quintana case: reopened in 1814, the death was revealed to have been a MURDER by neophytes (who had concealed it). This document is the initial (mistaken) 'natural death' finding — a key primary source for the cover-up. Resolved in Doc 177: the 5 neophyte murderers (Lino, Antonino, Vicente, Saturnino & Fulgencio[?]) were sentenced 10 Mar 1816 to 200 lashes + presidio. Orig. pp.694–736

author: José Cavenecia (capt./owner of the *Eagle*)
recipient: Gob. José Argüello; *diligencias* by Tte. José Mª Estudillo
themes: russia; america; astor; fort-ross; bodega; foreign-relations; ships; britain
summary: Capture of the American brig *Pedler* by the Spanish-chartered frigate *Eagle* in the ensenada de San Luis Obispo (26 Aug 1814). Cavenecia lured the *Pedler* by raising the U.S. flag, then hoisted the Spanish, fired 2 cannon-shots & boarded with 25 men. Papers seized: U.S. govt passport, property register, crew roll, accounts, a Russian passport from Nuevo Arcángel (Sitka) signed by Gen. Baranov, the cargo manifest & a drawing made aboard. Commander = Mr. Wilson P. Hunt (the Astorian / Pacific Fur Co.). Depositions: the *Pedler* came from Bodega, had carried Sandwich Islands goods for the Russian establishment, left some at Bodega, and sought the Russian brig *María* (alias *Lev*); pilot Gilbert Cárdenas[?] had loaded at Columbia [River] goods from 'el Gral. Ruso Granoff' for Bodega. Cargo inventory (Estudillo, 7 Sep): porcelain, glassware, sugar, coffee, beads, Russian wool, colored thread, painted cloth, shirts, pants, furs, hats, etc. Argüello (9 Sep) — since the *Pedler* truly sought the *María* to deliver the cargo, orders the captor to set sail and leave these coasts with cargo & papers. ⭐ Exposes the Baranov/RAC–Astor (Hunt) Pacific supply network through Bodega/Fort Ross. Orig. pp.197–219

themes: borradores; criminal; missions; indigenous; gabriel-moraga; quintana; mojave; bouchard; britain; america; defense; campaigns
summary: Long *borrador* register of Solá's outgoing letters/orders (1815–1822) — a major composite source. 1815–17 highlights: ⭐⭐⭐ the 5 neophyte murderers of Fr. Andrés Quintana (Lino, Antonino, Vicente, Saturnino & Fulgencio[?] of Sta Cruz) sentenced (10 Mar 1816) to 200 azotes + presidio (10 yrs the first two, 7 the others), at Sta Bárbara — resolution of the Quintana murder (cf. Doc 153); ⭐⭐ Solá commissions Gabriel Moraga, 25 men, to reconnoiter the sierra passes (18 Oct 1816); ⭐ a 3-masted brig built at San Juan Capistrano (Capt. Guillermo/William Reynolds); *cimarrón* pursuit & a San Gabriel uprising; SD soldiers Franco/Fermín/Fernando tried for the homicide of the Mayordomo Miguel; Vicente Nevado poisoning his wife; José Antº Tapia raping a neophyte; warship *San Carlos* + corbeta *Paz y Religión*; request for 40 mission Indians. 1819–20 highlights (the post-Bouchard reinforcement & Moraga's Mojave campaign): ⭐⭐ arrival at San Luis of the Mazatlán company — brig *Cossack* w/ 100 men + $10,000, Capt. Pablo de la Portilla (+ corbeta *Reina de los Ángeles*, *San Carlos*); promotions: Mariano Estrada (Tte.) & Manuel Gómez (artillery); ⭐⭐ Solá orders Gabriel Moraga's 1819 punitive campaign to the Mojave (Amajabat/Mongallune) ranchería — 35 cavalry + 15 Mazatlán infantry under Tte. Narciso Fabregat 'para conocer á los gentiles la bravura de las armas del Rey' (revenge-raids by Colorado-River gentiles had hit San Buenaventura, taking 5 Christian captives); ⭐ Moraga couldn't reach Amajabat for lack of pasture, halting at San Joaquín & Santa Ana de Aguablanca, returning via San Gabriel (Antonio Mª Lugo alcalde); to resume next summer. 1820–21 highlights: ⭐ English whaler at the coast (Aug 1820, 36 crew, 200 barrels whale oil, 'fragata inglesa ballenera,' Capt. Guillermo [William] —; her captain caroused with Capt. Francisco Mª Ruiz, who was removed from the SD command/habilitación 'por el uso de ciertos licores', replaced by José Mª Estudillo); foreigner-vigilance warnings; ⭐⭐ death of Cadet José Guadalupe Moraga at San Gabriel (Mar 1821); the Cocomaricopa 'general' sent home in peace (the gentiles hostile at San Buenaventura 2 yrs prior); murder of the Mazatlán soldier Pablo Serna & the neophyte Apolonia; continued Amajaba/Mojave alarms (gentiles plotting to draw off the Christians of San Gabriel & San Fernando); Estudillo's 40-yr service/seniority dispute; ⭐ the American schooner *Eagle* suspected of clandestine trade (Fabregat sold it goods — foreign commerce prohibited); the Cocomaricopa 'general' returned to his patria; command reshuffle (Ruiz→SD, Portilla→Sta Bárbara); ends w/ the death of Fr. Joaquín Pascual Nuez (San Gabriel, 1822). Orig. pp.152–350

author: José Joaquín de la Torre (copy by Gómez)

author: Ignacio Martínez (fiscal)

Early-ranchero cattle-theft (Nieto = future Santa Gertrudes/Los Nietos grantee). Orig. pp.452–75

author: Fr. José Senán
summary: Labor/discipline order re gentiles: 9 days' prison/cepo for gentiles who arrive at a *punta* and don't present to their padres; 15 days for *amos* who take on *agregados* without licence or don't report them to the ministers — born of the difficulty of forming *padrones*. Argüello accepts. Orig. pp.476–7

author: Ormaechea, Habilitado Gral.
summary: Factura/situado for the presidios (conductor Pedro Antº Serjano[?] & Cortés): Monterey $4,575/$5,109, San Diego $1,258/$9,046, Sta Bárbara $11,411/$12,222, SF $6,796/$7,253, artillery piquete $1,067/$1,210; total $32,107 (+ costs $34,840). Orig. pp.524–45

author: Fernandino friars (Colegio de San Fernando de México)
summary: 'Misiones de los RR.PP. Fernandinos' — mission-by-mission census of Alta California 1815–16: for each of the 21 missions (+ presidios & pueblos San José / Los Ángeles), the # of ministers, neophyte baptisms (mayores/menores), and total souls. Total Alta Cal. 1816 ≈ 22,432 souls; net increase 508. A key mission-demography table. Orig. p.546

author: Ignacio Martínez

author: Gob. Solá
themes: borradores; russia; kotzebue; fort-ross; bodega; france; britain; foreign-relations; gabriel-moraga; quintana; defense; campaigns
summary: SECOND *borrador* register of Solá's outgoing orders (1815–17) — the volume's richest Russian/foreign-relations stretch. Indigenous/campaign: ⭐⭐ Moraga's 1815 Palomas expedition (Solá orders Comte. Luis Argüello to mount 50 men—35 presidial + 15 inválidos/vecinos—and Gabriel Moraga to direct from Santa Cruz the campaign to the Ranchería de las Palomas); ⭐⭐⭐ Solá's Russian-trade policy query ('cuántas veces los Rusos han entrado… qué se les ha suministrado, cómo lo han pagado,' whether to continue/cut communication 'con otros Rusos, Anglo-Americanos ú otra Nación extranjera'); the Quintana assassins causa remitted to Gómez (Lino, Antonio, Vicente, Julián, Ambrosio, Andrés, Secundino, Fulgencio); the Juan Eliot de Castro chest sequestered (RAC agent, cf. C-A 12); Sgt. Soto's expedition. Russian/foreign maritime (1816–17): ⭐⭐⭐⭐ Kotzebue's *Rurik* at SF (Oct 1816) — 'franquee todos los auxilios que necesite el buque de Nueva Rusia, su Comandante Kossebue [Kotzebue]… que ancló ayer'; ⭐⭐⭐ the Russian ship *Suvorov* ('Serraroff,' Lazarev) at SF (21 Aug); ⭐⭐⭐ two Russians fled from 'el establecimiento situado arriba del Puerto de Bodega' (Fort Ross), declarations taken (Nov 1816); ⭐⭐⭐ the Russian war-chief Podushkin + an 'almost-resolved' decision to hand him a packet from St. Petersburg (Mar 1817); ⭐⭐ an anchored Russian ship's request to leave canoes/baidarkas at Sausalito to hunt (denied); ⭐⭐ the French corvette *Bordelais* (Roquefeuil), 200 t, for wood & water (Aug 1817, took deserters); ⭐⭐ an English NWC brig from the Columbia River (Aug 1816); ⭐⭐ defensive measures vs. the 'insurgentes de Buenos Aires' (pre-Bouchard, 1816: spike the battery guns if taken, missions to ready bows & arrows); ⭐ Fort San Joaquín works concluded (Gómez). Converges w/ C-A 12 (*Suvorov*, Elliot de Castro) & C-A 13 (Podushkin/*Chirikov*, Roquefeuil/*Bordelais*). Orig. pp.510–684

author: Lorenzo Estrada[?] (recording)
themes: russia; indigenous; otter; foreign-relations; ilmena; monterey
summary: 'Respuestas de Boris' — the interrogation of the captured Russian otter-hunter. He is Boris Tarasov, sailor, Russian of Novgorod; taken by comisionado Guillermo Cota (7 Oct); came from the 'islas fronterizas' to Sta Bárbara 7 days before capture; 3 times in the ensenada de San Pedro hunting otters, sheltering from storms; carried silk goods of his own. Questioned in Spanish (by Solá) and English (interpreter). ⭐⭐ Names Juan Elliot de Castro as captain of the *Ilmena*/'Hermina' (the RAC ship, cf. C-A 12) and John Cann; hadn't seen the *Ilmena* in 7 months. Refused to sign; signed a scrap of paper. ['Fin del tomo 46.'] Orig. pp.720–2

Mission ministers: San Miguel Juan Martín; San Antonio Pedro Cabot; La Soledad Antº Jayme; San Juan Bta. Felipe Arroyo de la Cuesta; San Luis Obispo Luis Antº Martínez & Antº Rodríguez; Sta Cruz Marcelino Marquínez & Jayme Escudé. Orig. pp.114–55

author: Comte. Luis de la Guerra
themes: russia; america; foreign-relations; ships; refugio; santa-barbara; military
summary: Sta Bárbara muster 1816 — embeds the Jan-1816 capture at the Refugio rancho: the comandante got a letter from Rancho del Refugio (Ortega's); Sgt. Carlos Carrillo warned that 2 ships were seen off 'la boca de las Islas' near the rancho; the alférez sortied that night with 14 men (troops, inválidos, vecinos). Over 14–20 Jan they captured 3 Americans, 2 Indians & Black prisoners + a boat ('apresado á Smith'); then 6 more Americans & the 2 captains; then the prize schooner — an otter-contraband crew taken at Refugio (links the C-A 12 Refugio/Eliot-de-Castro events). Company strength: Tte. de la Guerra, 3 sargtos, 6 cabos, 1 cadete, 56 soldados = 68 + Inválidos 28. Orig. pp.313–41

author: Luis Argüello
summary: Climate/health report: abundant pastures; the 11 missions' harvests regular; SF summer's 'furioso viento y neblina.' ⭐ Mission disease: gálico (syphilis), bloody dysentery, tisis (TB) & unknown maladies — some neophyte deaths. Orig. p.342

A long-run economic data series. Orig. pp.356–7

Ruled accidental, no culpability; but the fiscal urges a punishment 'para que… conozcan los demás alcaldes é indiada el que de ningún modo deben castigar… á sus súbditos' beyond what humanity allows. Notable on mission Indian-alcalde authority & violence. Orig. pp.88–96

El 9 se marcharon con rumbo para abajo.'* — Bouchard's two frigates at the Santa Bárbara roadstead 6 Dec 1818 (after Refugio on the 2nd), departing south the 9th. Orig. pp.507–18

José Argüello acted as governor after the death of José Joaquín de Arrillaga in 1814, until Pablo Vicente de Solá arrived (Sep–Oct 1815). Orig. p.530

Docs 133/138/230). Orig. p.590

author: Comte. José Mª Ruiz

author: Cadete Domingo Carrillo (juez fiscal; escribano Sgt. Joaquín Arce[?]); defensor Sgt. José Mª Pico
themes: criminal; indigenous; missions; san-diego; mission-abuse; resistance
summary: Causa criminal — the poisoning of Fr. Pedro Panto by his Indian cook Nazario (Mission San Diego). Cabo Antonio Guillén & Sgt. Mariano Mercado noticed the friar's soup tasted bitter (16 Nov); Panto vomited badly. Nazario confessed he'd carried a poison herb ('escoba amarga') for days and put it in the soup because Panto flogged him relentlessly — by his account 50 + 24 + 25 + 25 lashes over 15–16 Nov (200+ in two days 'sin dar graves motivos') — 'por ver si de este modo descansaba yo y los demás indios de la Misión'; testified 'ningún indio de la Misión lo quiere, ni menos los gentiles; y al Padre Sánchez tanto lo quieren los cristianos como los gentiles.' Defensor Pico: the herb wasn't lethal, Nazario 'un indio bruto,' driven by desperation. Fiscal Carrillo finds him not deserving of execution — asks 8 months' presidio at San Diego 'para escarmiento de los demás.' A landmark primary source on mission corporal punishment & indigenous resistance. Orig. pp.101–29

author: Comte. Ruiz
summary: Causa criminal — the neophyte José Mª (Mission San Diego) flogged to death a gentile woman ('Cuasislpi'), his lover, for squandering his goods & being unfaithful (1 Apr 1811). Fiscal: he 'took justice into his own hands,' a recent convert (1807) — 50 lashes + the 5 months already served; a gentile witness who beat her more harshly to get 50 lashes + 6 months. Indigenous-on-indigenous violence in the mission orbit. Orig. pp.131–56

author: fiscal Ignacio Martínez
summary: Causa criminal — 7 Mission San Diego neophytes (Alejo, Rafael, Antonio, Andrés, Gaspar, Joaquín + 1) tried for robbing the presidio warehouse (11 Mar). Sentences: Alejo 80 lashes (20/day × 4) + 5 yrs presidio w/ grillete at Monterey/SF; the others 80 lashes + 4 yrs; the 2 buyers of stolen goods 2 mo. at Punta de Guijarros; the receivers Dionisio & Andrés 50 lashes + 2 mo. Orig. pp.157–222

author: fiscal Alf. Ignacio Martínez
summary: Causa criminal vs. the inválido soldier Ramón Sotelo for 'blasphemous propositions about the Sacrament of Penance' (10 Oct). Friars Joaquín Pascual Nuez & Ramón Olbés testified (with P. Pres. Vicente Francisco de Sarría's licence) that his statements about confession were such that 'ni aún el protestante más calificado podría proferirlas en lo público.' Fiscal: his 'disparates' stem from ignorance of doctrine & frivolous *charlatanería* — sentence to public works at the SD presidio w/ grillete + public satisfaction to the pueblo of Los Ángeles + must learn doctrine & take Penance/Eucharist publicly. ⭐ A striking case of religious dissent/skepticism in 1817 California. Orig. pp.223–45
Mission minister: Fr. Juan Martín. Orig. p.255

author: José Mª Ruiz, Habilitado
summary: Pólvora sales $247. Orig. p.263

author: Ignacio Martínez, Habilitado
summary: Full SD habilitación account (Jun 1815→) — cargo $94,268 (incl. mission/particular debts $55,337), data $94,344; handed to Francisco Mª Ruiz. Orig. pp.300–4

author: Ruiz, Habilitado
summary: Cattle: net sales $635; 971 head on hand. Orig. p.310

author: Ruiz, Habilitado
summary: Diezmo net this year $303. Orig. pp.312–8

author: Ruiz, Habilitado
summary: Correos net this year $42. Orig. pp.319–21

author: Gob. Solá
summary: 'Instrucción pública': asks for a report on the girls' school at Santa Bárbara to see if the like can be done at Monterey; the boys' school is under Archuleta, doing it 'muy á mi gusto.' Education-history. Orig. p.347

author: Gob. Solá
summary: 'Destierro de vagos': resolved to 'limpiar esta provincia de algunas sanguijuelas que se mantienen en ella chupando la sangre de los pocos trabajadores que hay,' ordering certain individuals seized to be shipped out (vagrancy expulsion). Orig. pp.349–50

author: Gob. Solá
summary: 'Castigo por adulterio': re the scandal of the widow Anastasia Zúñiga (Los Ángeles) with the San-Gabriel-mission carpenter José Antonio Ramírez — Ramírez to acknowledge his obligations to mother & daughter & marry if possible (else his pay garnished for the child); jailed a month. The widow to be publicly shamed: head shaved, one eyebrow scraped, displayed bareface at Mass at San Gabriel, then 6 months' servitude & a 'religious life.' Brutal gendered moral discipline. Orig. pp.374–5

author: Gob. Solá
summary: 'Prisioneros rusos': Solá has orders to hand over the Russian prisoners & deserters, which he'll fulfill by shipping them off on the first vessel that appears — the resolution of the Russian-prisoner saga (repatriation, Oct 1818). Orig. p.377

author: Gob. Solá
summary: 'Medidas de precaución' — the Gyzelaar/Bouchard warning: approves de la Guerra's measures to defend the Santa Bárbara post 'ínterin quedan disueltas las sospechas… por la noticia que reservadamente comunicó á V. el Capitán del Bergantín Americano *Clarion*, Henrique [Henry] Gyzelaar'; approves removing the families & concentrating at the port 'con lo que se halle listo á todo.' The advance intelligence of Bouchard's coming, via Gyzelaar. Orig. pp.378, 387

author: Gob. Solá
summary: 'Desaprueba sus relaciones con los enemigos' — Solá rebukes de la Guerra for parleying with Bouchard. De la Guerra (oficio of the 7th) had parleyed under flag of truce with the enemy frigates at Sta Bárbara, agreeing to exchange the 3 prisoners Sgt. Carlos Carrillo took at the Rancho del Refugio for the prisoners Bouchard's frigates seized at the presidio, on 'la promesa que hizo… su caudillo Hipólito Bouchard, cesar toda acción hostil en cuanto comprende esta costa,' pending Solá's approval. Solá is 'escandalizado del atentado' of treating with pirates who fly no recognized flag; says that with the company + armed *vecindario* + Indian archers of San Isidro, Sta Bárbara, San Buenaventura & Purísima, de la Guerra showed 'poca ó ninguna disposición' to attack; orders him to keep vigilant & aid San Diego. The central document of the Bouchard prisoner-exchange controversy. Orig. pp.383

author: Gob. Solá
summary: Solá, 'absorto,' tells the friar that Capt. de la Guerra committed 'el atentado de parlamentar con ellos (los piratas)' & agreed the prisoner exchange. Orig. p.383

author: Gob. Solá
summary: 'Sobre canje de prisioneros con Bouchard; pena á Molina; desagrado con el Tte. Ignacio Martínez.' Of the supposed 3 prisoners the enemy claimed, only one (Molina) was actually exchanged; suspecting Molina may have gone over to Bouchard voluntarily, Solá sentences him to 6 yrs presidio in chains (ración, no pay) + 100 public lashes, the company & vecindario assembled, the sentence read aloud 'para escarmiento.' Orders the 20 Monterey soldiers back; voices displeasure with Tte. Ignacio Martínez for his tardiness; lookouts to track the enemy's course. Orig. pp.384

author: Gob. Solá
summary: 'Sobre los buques de Bouchard': approves de la Guerra's going to San Pedro with 25 men, Bouchard's two ships having put in there on 13 Dec — with that force + the San Diego company + armed *vecinos* + the San Gabriel escort, ready to fire on the enemy if they landed; the San Diego commander prepared the battery with hot-shot ('bala roja') to receive them. The San Pedro/southern phase of the raid. Orig. p.385

author: Gob. Solá
summary: Bouchard's insurgent frigates sighted heading to the rada de San Juan Capistrano (13 Dec); Santiago Argüello dispatched to watch them; de la Guerra expected there w/ reinforcement; if the enemy breaks the pact, Solá hopes he'll be 'bien escarmentado.' Orig. p.385

author: Gob. Solá
summary: 'Preparativos contra Bouchard': approves taking Fr. Luis Martínez to San Juan Capistrano where the rebels had anchored; with the SD troop + de la Guerra's men + *vecinos* + Indian archers, hopes to make the pirates 'respetar… las tropas del Gobierno'; set up horse-relays. Orig. p.385

author: Gob. Solá
summary: Acks Argüello's reports (15–16 Dec) of the action at San Juan Capistrano with the rebels; 3 enemy soldiers + a drummer presented themselves with arms (promised no vejación if sincere); Sgt. Anastasio Carrillo arrived with 23 soldiers + 26 vecinos. Orig. p.386

author: Gob. Solá
summary: The 3 enemy soldiers + drummer who surrendered to be interned at San Fernando (away from the coast) 'esta clase de sujetos no están bien cerca de la playa.' Orig. p.386

author: Gob. Solá
themes: foreign-relations; britain; america; astoria; columbia; monterey
summary: An English warship (corvette) at Monterey since 3 Nov seeking refreshments — 'Viene al Río Columbia con la comisión de verificar su entrega á los Americanos, á cuyo fin conduce á los Comisionados por los Estados Unidos', sailing on the 10th–11th. The restitution of Astoria/Fort George to the U.S. under the Treaty of Ghent (1818) — likely HMS *Blossom*. Orig. p.388

author: Gob. Solá
summary: Approves moving the American prisoner from Mission San Miguel to the presidio, where 'podrá trabajar en la recomposición de armas de fuego, y construcción de machetes' under guard. (The gap-sample's '150 machetes' context.) Orig. p.388

author: Gob. Solá
summary: Acks the 2 pardoned insurgents. Orig. p.391

author: Gob. Solá
summary: 'Sobre el preso Molina y sus relaciones con los insurgentes': Pedro Molina's answers (re a 10 Feb circular) about joining the insurgents when they attacked the Monterey presidio on 22 Nov 1818 conflict with the 4 men who came over from Bouchard (pardoned) — 6 yrs presidio w/ grillete for Molina. Confirms Bouchard's attack on Monterey, 22 Nov 1818. Orig. p.391

author: Gob. Solá
summary: A schooner from Panamá reached San Diego, having been attacked by the insurgents at the Islas Marías. Orig. p.391

author: Gob. Solá
summary: 'Muertes por indios del Colorado': Colorado-River Indians killed Rufino Leyva[?], Mariano Cota & a neophyte at San Buenaventura (30 May 1819) (per Fr. José Señán). Sgt. Anastasio Carrillo sortied with 12 soldiers + 3 artillerymen & a *pedrero* (swivel-gun) to seize the killers. The revenge-raid context for Moraga's 1819 Mojave campaign. Orig. p.391, 399

author: Gob. Solá
summary: 'Captura de indios': wants a lookout on the Colorado-River route; the 4 prisoners taken by Sgt. Anastasio Carrillo held at Sta Bárbara until a Spanish ship can deport them. Orig. pp.399–400

author: Gob. Solá
themes: indigenous; gabriel-moraga; personnel; bouchard; defense; colorado-river
summary: Multiple: the Colorado raid (of 21 Indians, 11 killed, 10 fled per Guerra; '10 attackers' per Señán); the Viceroy, after the Monterey attack, named Solá Coronel de Milicias Provinciales; reinforcements coming (Fr. Carlos w/ troops/artillery/money from San Blas; the brig *Cossack* w/ 100 men from Mazatlán); ⭐⭐ Alféreces Ignacio Martínez & Gabriel Moraga promoted to Teniente (Martínez → SF company, Moraga → Sta Bárbara company); promotions of José Estrada & Manuel Gómez. Solá expects relief from his governorship. Orig. pp.401–3

author: Gob. Solá
summary: 'Prohibición a los indios de andar a caballo': bans the 'escandaloso uso de andar á caballo' by the neophytes of San Fernando & San Gabriel (except *vaqueros*) — 25 lashes for violators, then returned to their mission. Orig. p.407

author: Gob. Solá
summary: Writing to the Viceroy praising the missions' & ministers' service when the 'insurgentes de Buenos Aires' attacked Monterey (Nov 1818), especially Fr. Luis Antonio Martínez. Orig. p.407

author: Gob. Solá
summary: The Mazatlán reinforcement company: landed at San Luis (Baja Cal.) 6 Aug, sailed from Mazatlán on the brig *Cossack* 14 Jul w/ $10,000 + sealed paper; Capt. Pablo de la Portilla w/ 2 Ttes., 2 Alf. & 95 men (fusil/bayonet/cartridge-box); marching overland to San Diego. Orig. p.413

author: Gob. Solá
summary: Supply crisis: arrival at Monterey of the warship *San Carlos* + merchant schooner *Nueva Reyna de los Ángeles* w/ 100 men but 'sin más auxilios… que el de dos pagas' — keeps Solá sleepless; no funds/artillery received; 400 useless saber-blades returned; wants de la Guerra to carry an 'enérgica representación' to the Viceroy on the province's critical state. Orig. pp.414–5

author: Gob. Solá
summary: 'Comisión… Expedición contra indios': commissions de la Guerra to go to México to represent the province's crisis (a general power from all companies to audit the Habilitado Gral.; $100/mo.); orders Teniente Gabriel Moraga to lead the expedition against the [Colorado/Mojave] Indians, taking 100+ armed mission Indians from San Buenaventura, San Fernando & San Gabriel; 20–25 men from San Diego to combine w/ Sta Bárbara's. Orig. pp.416–7

author: Gob. Solá
summary: Import duties on the frigate *Nueva Reyna de los Ángeles* — $192 of goods taken from her captain José Bandini + $1,145 from a Bandini libranza to be set off. Orig. p.419

author: Gob. Solá
summary: 'Comisión a México; refuerzo para Sta Bárbara': sends the general powers; orders Capt. Pablo de la Portilla to detach 1 Tte., 1 Alf. & 45 men of the Mazatlán company to garrison Santa Bárbara. Orig. p.419

author: Gob. Solá
summary: 'Expedición contra indios': 30 men from Sta Bárbara + 30 from San Diego to unite under Tte. Gabriel Moraga for the expedition into the rancherías sheltering fugitive Christian Indians; the 45 Mazatlán men reached SD 16 Sep; rations prepared. Orig. pp.420–1

author: Gob. Solá
summary: Instruction letter to Moraga for the 1819 expedition against the 'indios prófugos': 'deseoso de dar una castigada general á los gentiles por… abrigar en sus rancherías á los cristianos huidos de las misiones,' forms an expedition of 60 men under Moraga (30 Sta Bárbara + 30 San Diego), asks Moraga's written plan; coordinates another party of 25 under another officer, a SF party of 20–25 under Sgt. Sánchez via San José, and a launch with a cannon by water — a multi-pronged interior campaign. Asks where the SD & SB parties should rendezvous. Orig. pp.421–2

author: Gob. Solá
summary: Orders him to embark on the *Nueva Reyna de los Ángeles* for San Blas, then Guadalajara & México. Orig. pp.422–3

author: Gob. Solá
summary: Solá's gubernatorial dates: appointed Gov. 31 Dec 1814; oath & possession given by Tte. José de la Cruz (per Viceroy Calleja's orders) 31 Mar 1815. Orig. pp.423–4

author: Gob. Solá
summary: Supply-crisis complaints: urgent letters to the Viceroy (6 & 24 Sep) for money; the *San Carlos* & *Nueva Reyna* failed to bring the promised 400 muskets & 500 swords; only 5 six-pdrs + four-pdrs received; the 100 reinforcement men were 'sacados de las cárceles y cogidos en leva' (jailbirds & press-ganged) — 'de ellos no se espera sino dolores y resultas'; the 2-ship expedition cost the Real Erario 'muy cerca de $60,000'; the 400 saber-blades came without hilts. Vivid on the dysfunction of the post-Bouchard reinforcement. Orig. pp.412, 425

author: Gob. Solá
summary: 'Campaña contra indios': approves the province making its expedition 'en los términos de su demarcación'; San Diego to send a force visiting the points Moraga designates; once the 45 Mazatlán men reach Sta Bárbara, Moraga sets out (command of SB arms left to a Tte. Argüello). ⭐⭐ The Monterey party under Tte. José Mª Estudillo (guide Sgt. José Dolores Pico as *práctico*) to set out 10–15 Oct, enter via San Miguel, scout the laguna de Ugarra[?], Telame, and the San Gabriel river, then return. A multi-pronged interior (Tulares) sweep. Orig. pp.425–30

author: Gob. Solá
summary: Each presidio to allow de la Guerra a $25 monthly allowance for his México mission. Orig. p.434

author: Gob. Solá
summary: Cannot grant his retirement to México yet — the replacement friars (from Orizaba & San Fernando) haven't arrived. Orig. pp.432–3

author: Gob. Solá
summary: Activate the militia artillerymen of his district under arms, drawing pay from today. Orig. p.435

author: Gob. Solá
summary: 'Molina y su castigo': the *paisano* Isidro Molina [Doc 271 has 'Pedro Molina' — name variance per Savage] for joining the insurgents at the Monterey presidio during the attack of 22–25 Nov 1818 — sentenced to 6 yrs presidio at Sta Bárbara in public works (ración, no pay). Confirms Bouchard's multi-day occupation of Monterey, 22–25 Nov 1818. Orig. p.435

author: Gob. Solá
summary: Solá asks de la Guerra to lobby influential people in México to relieve him of 'esta carga que me es muy penosa'; has asked the Viceroy for an Intendancy or a Real Hacienda / Cajas post. Orig. pp.438–9

author: Gob. Solá
summary: Acks Moraga's report (18th): Tte. Narciso Fabregat reached Sta Bárbara w/ an alférez & 45 men; Moraga to set out w/ 30 of his company on the ordered campaign. De la Guerra presumed embarked on the *Nueva Reyna de los Ángeles*. The 1819 Moraga expedition launches. Orig. p.441

author: Gob. Solá
summary: Keep his troop drilled in arms. Orig. p.442

author: Gob. Solá
summary: Order for the Amajaba (Mojave) campaign: the ranchería de Amajaba threatened to attack Missions San Gabriel & San Fernando; Moraga to take 50 men (35 cavalry from both presidios + 15 Mazatlán under Fabregat) + the armament Tte. Francisco Mª Ruiz brought + 4 artillerymen + a cannon (metralla y bala), march at once to the Mojave ranchería, make the *capitanejos* surrender the fugitive Christian Indians, then leave garrisons of 15 men at Missions San Gabriel & San Fernando. Orig. pp.442–3

author: Gob. Solá
summary: The Mazatlán infantry alférez stationed at Sta Bárbara was Ignacio Delgado. Orig. p.443

author: Gob. Solá
themes: gabriel-moraga; indigenous; missions; runaways; tulares; campaigns
summary: Results of Moraga's 1819 campaign: Moraga captured fugitive Christian Indians (cimarrones) + 11 gentiles, sent to Sta Bárbara. Solá's sentences per named runaway by length of absence: Sebastián & Cristóbal 2 yrs; Ignacio, Cayetano, Diego 2 mo. + 3×25 lashes; Juan Mª (La Purísima) 2 yrs; Olegario (Sta Inés) 6 mo. + lashes; Felipe (San Miguel) 6 mo. + 25 lashes; the 11 gentile 'harborers' (old men) 25 lashes × 3 days + 1 yr (to be Christianized). ⭐⭐ Per de la Guerra, gentile chiefs guided Moraga's expedition, extracted 11 Christians from the laguna de Buenavista (Buena Vista Lake, Tulares), offered to help soldiers enter 'the islands' — Moraga paid them $2–3 each. Key on mission flight/runaways & the Tulares. Orig. pp.445–6

author: Gob. Solá
summary: The Mojave expedition FAILED to reach the Amajaba ranchería — 'por la absoluta imposibilidad en que se hallan esos caminos, pastos, y agua para la caballada'; Fabregat & Moraga returned to Sta Bárbara 14 Dec. (Confirms Doc 177.) Orig. p.453

author: Gob. Solá
summary: The expeditions had poor results, but Sgt. Sánchez's Santa Cruz-direction expedition was 'muy brillante' — recommends him for the grade of Alférez. Orig. p.455

author: Gob. Solá
summary: 'Indios malhechores castigados': praises Antonio Ignacio Ávila (regidor of Los Ángeles) & vecino Juan Nepomuceno Alvarado for killing the principal ringleader of the 'diéguino' raiders who hid much of the game/stock of Simí (3 others wounded & disarmed); orders monthly patrols by 4–5 vecinos 'para su total exterminio.' Settler-Indian violence near Simí/LA. Orig. pp.464–5

author: Gob. Solá
summary: 'Pobreza en Cal.': poverty & misery in California 'subiendo en grado'; nothing to trade with foreigners for want of ships from Peru or México; vigilance vs. insurgent ships. Orig. pp.465–6

author: Gob. Solá
summary: 'Venta de licor': only one tavern at SB (established by José Argüello); prevent disorders & no liquor to be sold to Indians or neophytes. Orig. p.466

author: Luis Argüello, Habilitado
summary: Full SF company account 1817 (cargo $47,213 incl. mission debts $10,925; data $47,409; balance $195). Orig. pp.471–4

author: Luis Argüello, Habilitado
summary: Cattle: net sales $303; 586 head on hand 31 Dec 1817. Orig. pp.497–8

author: Argüello
summary: Climate/health: abundant rains; the 4 SF missions' harvests regular; ⭐ neophyte disease (tisis, gálico, disentería de sangre); livestock fecund. Orig. p.504

author: Comte. José de la Guerra
summary: Sta Bárbara muster 1817: 68 + Inválidos 27. Orig. pp.509–32

author: Comte. Francisco Mª Ruiz
summary: San Diego muster 1817: 72 + Artillería 4 + Inválidos 23. Orig. pp.543–85

author: Comte. Luis Argüello
summary: SF muster 1817: 70 + Artillería 5 + Inválidos 11. Orig. pp.533–609

author: Comte. José Mª Estudillo
summary: Militia artillery on duty (camped at the Rancho de la Real Hacienda by Solá's order): cabos 2º José de Jesús Vallejo, Quintín Ortega + 15 soldiers. Orig. pp.638–9

author: Comte. José Mª Estudillo
summary: Monterey muster 1818: 84 + 4 + Artillería 5 + Inválidos 28. Orig. pp.633–704

author: Ignacio Martínez, juez fiscal (for Comte. Luis Argüello)
themes: criminal; indigenous; missions; santa-cruz; quintana; resistance
summary: Causa criminal — the 1820 interrogation of the neophyte Alberto (Mission Sta Cruz) re the murder of Fr. Andrés Quintana. Alberto's sworn testimony: the Indian Quirico invited him 'á ayudar á matar al Padre,' luring him to the mission huerta at prayer-time on the pretext of stealing pears, where the other conspirators were gathered; the Indian Andrés confirmed they meant to kill the Padre; Alberto says he refused ('que él no se metía en nada'), left for home, learned next morning the Padre was dead; confessed he did wrong not to warn the guard/mayordomo; fled to the *Monte*/sierra when the others were arrested, fed once by his son via Fr. Marcelino, told to go to Punta de Año Nuevo (the mission rancho). A rich primary indigenous-testimony source on the Quintana assassination (cf. Docs 153/177/188). Orig. pp.707–10

author: fiscal Santiago Argüello
summary: Causa criminal — the Indian Serafín accused the vecino Juan Mª Tapia of raping his wife (Serafia Paris[?], who then died, attributed to Tapia's mistreatment). The *dictamen* states Indian testimony is inadmissible: *'á los indios no se les puede llamar testigos mayores de toda excepción, pues aún siete de estos (según el Sr. Torre Granada) no hacen una semiplena probanza.'* Sentence: only 6 months' public works + grillete. Key on indigenous legal status. Orig. pp.723–79

author: Comte. Luis Argüello
summary: SF muster 1818: 72 + Artillería 5 + Inválidos 11. Orig. pp.798–9

author: Gob. Solá
summary: Recruitment: go to Los Ángeles, pick 12–15 young men (voluntarily) from the comisionado's list for the cavalry, bring them to Monterey with their own horses. Orig. pp.801–2

author: José Joaquín de la Torre, Habilitado
summary: [Account]. Orig. pp.803–9

author: fiscal Santiago Argüello
summary: Causa criminal — the neophyte Juan Bautista, who took church asylum at San Gabriel, accused of killing a Christian Indian (Lerme[?]) with arrows; both were mission runaways, the killing over a debt of 'tres reales de abalorios' (3 reales of beads). Defense (the 'mercader viandante' Juan Salvador Astorga): 'un cristiano nuevo, rudo, y loco.' Fiscal asks to contest the church asylum (per Benedict XIV's exclusion of such crimes). Orig. pp.810–39

author: Alf. Santiago Argüello (escribano José Antº Pico)
summary: 'Declaraciones de insurgentes de la expedición de Bouchard' — depositions of the 4 captured/surrendered crew of Bouchard's frigates, revealing the multinational makeup of the expedition: (1) Pedro Saldívar, 22, of Buenos Aires, cabo; (2) Juan de la Rosa, 27, of Scotland, labrador; (3) Nicolás Blanco, 21, of Santa Fé, soldier of the frigate *Consecuencia*; (4) Mateo José Pascual, ~25, native of Africa, panadero, baptized in Buenos Aires. All questioned on Isidro Molina — uniformly testify he was drunk at the Monterey presidio, riding a white horse, not a prisoner, who came over voluntarily ('se ha venido á pasar á presentar'), with a rumor he was a spy (*vigía*) sent by the Governor; couldn't say if he landed at the Ortegas' rancho or went ashore willingly at Sta Bárbara. Each signed with a cross (illiterate). A remarkable primary source on Bouchard's crew & the Molina case. Orig. pp.~256–61

author: José de la Guerra
summary: 'Ataque sobre buques; estado indefenso de las provincias': will keep precaution with every approaching ship lest it carry the expedition prepared by Lord Cochrane & Sir Robert Wilson against the King's American dominions; has only 25–30 men; stresses the presidios' defenseless state on 'esta abierta y dilatada costa'; from 1 May the militia-artillerymen to rotate to the presidio monthly. Orig. pp.853–5

author: José de la Guerra, Habilitado
summary: Bulas net 1817–18: $108. Orig. p.867

author: Sgt. Juan de Ortega, comisionado
summary: Averiguación on the jail-escape of Ramón Sotelo (the blasphemy-case inválido, Doc 248) from the LA pueblo jail 'sin haber sido visto ni sentido'; proved the pueblo guard was negligent. Orig. pp.870–82

author: Gob. Solá
summary: 'Compañías presidiales y su costo': detailed company-cost table — Monterey $23,701, SF $19,092, Sta Bárbara $19,592, San Diego $20,187, artillery detachment $2,736, gov. salary $4,000; total $91,778 (data $87,071). Pay-scale: Capitán $1,500/yr, Tte. $550, Alférez $400, Cirujano $1,000, Sargento $262.50, Cabo $225, Soldado $217.50, Sangrador $360, Carpintero $180. Valuable military-fiscal data. Orig. p.896

author: Manuel Gómez, Comte.
summary: Militia-artillery company (distributed across the 4 presidios & missions): 1 cabo 1º + 4 cabos 2º + 57 soldados = 62. Orig. pp.913–4

author: Manuel Gómez, Comte.
summary: 'Artillería y pertrechos — Existentes en Cal.' — a complete province-wide artillery inventory (1818): 58 pieces by caliber (24/12/8/6/4/3/2/1-pdr) & metal (bronze/iron), located at the Castillo de San Joaquín (SF) (3×24, 8×8 bronze, etc.), Castillo de San Carlos (Monterey), Santa Bárbara presidio & baluarte, San Diego, Los Ángeles, Pueblo San José, and the missions (San Diego, Sta Inés, San Fernando, San Luis Rey). 3 useless, several *desgranados*; the 24-pdrs & SF iron 12-pdr corroded; gun-carriages (cureñas — campaña/plaza/marina) in poor repair 'por falta de herraje.' A comprehensive ordnance census — major for presidio-defense/decay. Orig. p.915

author: Comte. Francisco Mª Ruiz
summary: San Diego muster 1818: 72 + 2 + Artillería 4 + Inválidos 21. Orig. pp.917–1076

author: Sgt. Guillermo Cota, comisionado
summary: Causa criminal — Cástor Cota[?], Rafael García, Pedro Arce & the Ibarras accused of stealing tallow (*sebo*) from vecino Antº Ávila at Los Ángeles. Orig. pp.934–81

author: Francisco Mª Ruiz, Habilitado
summary: Full SD habilitación account (from Ignacio Martínez, Dec 1817): cargo $104,457 (incl. debts to presidios/missions/particulars $74,555 + $3,261 owed to Loreto); data $102,995; balance $638; handed to Santiago Argüello. Orig. pp.992–4

author: Ruiz, Habilitado
summary: Libranzas on the Habilitado Gral.: correos 1817 $42, diezmos $303, Mission San Juan Capistrano $1,000 = $1,345. Orig. p.1000

author: Comte. Francisco Mª Ruiz
summary: Population census of the San Diego district 1818 — presidio + Missions San Luis Rey/San Diego/San Juan Capistrano/San Gabriel, by sex & age; totals 2,793/2,310/1,011/960 (gran total ≈ 7,074); broken down by caste (españoles, indios 2,645+, mestizos, color quebrado) & marital status (8 ecclesiastics; casados 1,344; viudos; solteros). Each mission had 2 missionaries; sínodos $400 each = $3,200, paid from the ex-Jesuit 'temporalidades' (Pious Fund). Valuable demography. Orig. pp.1002–5

author: fiscal Santiago Argüello
themes: criminal; indigenous; missions; gender; san-diego; native-medicine
summary: Causa criminal — the neophyte Antonio/Rafael Chales flogged his wife María Gabriela to death (Mission San Diego). The escort cabo Pedro Lobo found her body whip-marked, 'de la cintura abajo, en los muslos, toda desollada, enseñando la carne viva.' Chales's confession: he beat her with a 'real doblado' + 25 lashes for a paper given by a gentile Indian she'd slept with 3 nights; she fell ill, he tried the native sweat-cure (heated hole + green herb, *frazada*) — she died Monday morning. Fiscal: among the Diéguinos, fear of punishment leads them to kill rather than leave a victim alive, then flee or take sanctuary; flogging-abuse 'se va haciendo común entre ellos.' Orig. pp.1077–1104

author: Gob. Solá
recipient: Capt. Luis Argüello (investig. by Argüello, escribano cabo José Berreyesa)
place: Rancho de la Real Hacienda / Sta Cruz
themes: bouchard; missions; santa-cruz; branciforte; criminal; plunder
summary: The Branciforte sack of Mission Santa Cruz during the Bouchard scare. Fr. Ramón Olbés complained the Branciforte comisionado & villagers committed 'atentados' on the mission's property. When Bouchard's force took Monterey (23 Nov 1818), Olbés evacuated the mission toward Sta Clara; the comisionado Joaquín Buelna had been ordered to remove the mission's goods (under inventory) before the enemy could sack them — instead, the inválido Joaquín Castro found Buelna & most of the Branciforte vecindario sacking the mission: forcing the main gate with axes, breaking the warehouse windows, carrying off the goods; returning 6–7 days later they found it 'enteramente saqueada,' doors/windows/stores broken, the church profaned — a crucifix, the image of San Luis & the Santo Niño 'hechos pedazos,' excrement on canes in the sacristy. A vivid, well-known episode. The testimony is conflicting: the comisionado Joaquín Buelna's defense quotes the Gov.'s actual written order ('hará V. que pase con algunos á destruir aquellos intereses para que los enemigos no se apoderen de ellos') and blames the mayordomo Castro & 60 mission Indians for the disorderly sack; villager José Mª Salazar says Castro broached the liquor barrels; some say the Indians (not the villagers) broke the crucifix and that San José/the Santo Niño were undamaged. Argüello sent all the declarations to the Gov. (18 Nov 1819). Orig. pp.3–26

author: Frs. Felipe Arroyo de la Cuesta & Vicente Francisco de Sarría
summary: Re the Sta Cruz sack: 'No pretendamos… nuestra vindicta de pena de sangre, sino la satisfacción á la misión é individuos damnificados.' Notes Sarría's prefecture of the missions has ended; Fr. Mariano Payeras becomes Prefect. Orig. p.27

author: Gob. Solá
summary: Second investigation of the Sta Cruz sack: Estrada examines more Branciforte residents (Julián Ríos, Timoteo Vázquez, Luis García, Antº Ortega, Martín Vela, José Archuleta, Vicente Aguila, Dionisio Ríos, Antº Montero…) — all confirm comisionado Buelna's version of 23 Nov. Orig. pp.42–58

author: Gob. Solá
themes: bouchard; missions; santa-cruz; branciforte; criminal; plunder; gender
summary: Third investigation — the stolen trunk & household concealment. Adds the theft of a baúl of valuables Fr. Olbés entrusted for transport to Sta Clara — José Antº Robles & son Nieves stole it after getting Martín Ortega drunk; Ortega sold some contents (→ to be charged & arrested). Solá orders interrogating the mission Indians (about what they & the mayordomo hid) and the villagers' wives (concealed goods, cloth cut into clothes, items sold & to whom). Testimony: the wives (Pascuala Lorenzana, María Remigia Vázquez…) deny concealment; the Indian Silvestre and soldier José Mª Martín Ortega testify the mayordomo Castro directed the Indians to break doors with axes/bars and loot the warehouse & church ornaments, hauling goods to the laguna del Sargento. A multi-party plunder where villagers, Indians & mayordomo blame each other. Closes with the stolen-trunk strand: the Robles family (José Antº, Nieves, Rafael) sold the loot (a saya, black stockings, beads, blue-baize trousers) in San Francisco. Orig. pp.60–94

author: Gob. Solá
summary: 'Averiguación sobre expresiones del Padre Olbés en el púlpito' — a church-state conflict. Per Florentín Machuca's letter to Buelna, Fr. Ramón Olbés preached (28 Feb) that 'el Sr. Gob., oficiales, sargentos, cabos, y soldados todos se tapaban, y que aquí no había justicia' (the whole hierarchy covered up the mission theft), threatening to deny confession to the sick & burial in consecrated ground. Solá orders Estrada to investigate and make Olbés justify in writing 'expresiones tan escandalosas' from 'aquel paraje tan sagrado.' Witnesses (Machuca, the mayordomo Castro, Pedro Chabolla) confirm the sermon. Orig. pp.98–107

author: Comte. Luis Argüello
summary: SF presidial muster 1819 — reflects the 1819 promotions: Capt. Luis Argüello, Tte. Gabriel Moraga, Alf. Santiago Argüello, 2 sargtos, 8 cabos, 1 cadete, 57 soldados = 71; Artillería (Subtte. grad. Manuel Gómez + 4) = 5; Inválidos 12. Orig. pp.114–52

author: Maitorena, Habilitado
summary: Rentas especiales: diezmos (31 Dic 1819) $623, correos $90 + $48, bulas (1818–19) $305 = paid via libranzas on Habilitado Gral. interino Gervasio Argüello $1,066. Orig. p.157

author: Maitorena, Habilitado
summary: Cuenta de misiones: libranzas (31 Dic 1819) to pay arrears: La Purísima $12,688, Sta Inés $8,270 … Orig. p.157

author: Comte. Luis Argüello
summary: Causa criminal (by Solá's order): Tte. Moraga investigates the theft of Fr. Olbés's trunk by José Antº Robles & son Nieves (& Martín Ortega). Nieves Robles confesses he broke the trunk on Ortega's & his father's orders ('echando en una frazada lo que pudo'), hid it at Las Espuelas. (Moraga as juez fiscal.) Orig. pp.~

author: Comte. José de la Guerra (Habilitado Maitorena)
themes: military; rosters; gabriel-moraga; indigenous; mojave; santa-barbara
summary: Sta Bárbara muster 1819: Capt. de la Guerra, Tte. Gabriel Moraga, Alf. Maitorena, 3 sargtos, 6 cabos, 1 cadete (de la Guerra), 56 soldados = 69 + Inválidos 27. ⭐⭐ Embeds a certified burial record (Fr. José Senán): Mariano Cota (Sta Bárbara soldier, on sentinel duty) atrociously killed during High Mass on Pentecost 1819 by Amajahua/Mojave gentiles from the Río Colorado (20–21 then held in the guardhouse), and cabo Rufino Leyva, killed the same day — at San Buenaventura. The Mojave attack that triggered Moraga's 1819 campaign (cf. Doc 273). Orig. pp.227–74

author: José de la Guerra
summary: Officer swap: Tte. Ignacio Martínez → San Francisco; Tte. Gabriel Moraga → Santa Bárbara (1819). Orig. p.297

author: Antº Guartara[?]
summary: Ordnance to Monterey (via José Bandini): 5 iron 6-pdr cannon w/ carriages, etc., + 50 [units] powder. Orig. pp.298–9

author: Antº Guartara
summary: Armament to Monterey on the brig *San Carlos*: 400 sabers + 3 national plaza-flags (the sabers Solá complained were hiltless/useless). Orig. p.303

author: Gob. Solá
summary: 'Aprobación del Virrey': the Viceroy Conde del Venadito (Apodaca) (oficio 22 Mar), pleased by Solá's 13-Dec report on the insurgent (Buenos Aires) frigates' attack, thanks the California troops & vecinos for their valor, honor & fidelity in the Bouchard defense in the King's & his own name; published in the Gaceta extraordinaria de México (24 Mar). Official recognition of the Bouchard defense. Orig. p.305

author: Gob. Solá
summary: 'Vigilancia': ships seen off San Diego — could be the awaited San Blas troop ships or enemies. Orig. p.311

author: Gob. Solá
themes: defense; ordnance; machetes; missions; indigenous; logistics; bouchard
summary: 'Preparativos de defensa' — the '150 machetes' order. With Fr. Prefect Mariano Payeras, Solá ordered 150 machetes + 150 cartridge-boxes made to arm the Monterey troops & some vecinos, for the shortage of swords; 2 model machetes made at Mission Soledad sent to the missions as patterns (each mission to make a share). PLUS a horse-relay defense plan: each named *paraje* between the missions (San Antonio–San Miguel, San Luis Obispo–Purísima, San Buenaventura–San Fernando, San Gabriel–San Juan Capistrano) to keep ≥150 horses for rapid troop remounts against the 'traitors' (Bouchard). Order grew to 200 machetes; 40 skilled Indian bowmen to be named at each mission (30 San Luis Obispo, 16 Soledad/San Carlos/Sta Cruz, 20 San Juan Bautista) as auxiliaries, each mission to elect an Indian 'capitán.' Includes a mission-by-mission machete/cartridge-box production quota (e.g., SF 10, Sta Clara 10, San Gabriel 24… = 150 + 150). Orig. pp.301–15

author: Comte. José Mª Estudillo
summary: Monterey muster 1819: Tte. Estudillo, Tte. grad. José Estrada, Cirujano Quijano, cadete José de la Torre, 2 sargtos, 10 cabos, 65 soldados = 81 + 4 + Artillería 5 + Inválidos 28. Orig. pp.317–373

author: Ignacio Martínez, Tte. de caballería, juez fiscal
summary: Causa criminal — incest/rape by a stepfather (CLOSED). Per comisionado Manuel Rodríguez's report, Cornelio Rosales (of Branciforte; 41 años, natural de San Luis Obispo, casado con María Luisa Robles[?]) impregnated his stepdaughter María Antonia Sepúlveda and committed estupro on his goddaughter (hijada de bautismo) María Josefa; younger sisters María Paula & María Inés also implicated, the children sleeping together. Timoteo Vásquez offered to marry María Antonia & 'take the blame.' Conflicting careos: María Antonia & María Paula affirm Cornelio violated them; Rosales & María Josefa deny. Dictamen fiscal (Martínez): estupro 'plenamente justificado,' aggravated by mal ejemplo & público escándalo 'abriendo las puertas á muchos para que sigan sus maldades'; recommends Rosales removed from the Province — 4 años de trabajos públicos con grillete al pie at the presidio de San Diego. Penances for the girls (azotes by their mother's hand, in the girls' room at Mission Santa Cruz): María Josefa 50 azotes; María Antonia 50 azotes + 1 month arrest; Paulita 25 azotes; the mother a stern reprimand. Tied to the 'revolución de los insurgentes' (Bouchard period, Dec 1818) at Buena Vista rancho; midwife examinations confirmed abuse. Orig. pp.348–368.

author: Alférez José Mª Maitorena, juez fiscal; under Tte. José de la Guerra y Noriega
summary: Causa criminal — uxoricide by a neophyte (CLOSED). On 4 Aug 1817 Cabo Antonio Olivera of the Sta Buenaventura escort reported the indio Gorgonio had killed his wife, buried her, then taken refuge in the church (iglesia) [ecclesiastical asylum]; the indio Vitorio named accomplice (relayed by soldier José Vicente Pico). Body found 'con 5 heridas y la cabeza muy maltratada.' 5 Aug Tte. Guerra ordered Maitorena's sumaria. Defender Ignacio Martínez 10 Nov 1818; returned to fiscal 2 Mzo 1819; Gorgonio cited por edictos. Dictamen fiscal (Martínez): neophyte Gorgonio confessed strangling his own wife, afternoon of 3 Aug 1817 → digno de severo castigo; urges the Tribunal weigh 'el derramamiento de la sangre inocente'; notes a parallel atrocious killing at Mission San Fernando, 22 leguas distant, plus others 'quedado impunes' for difficulty of proving culprits — argues exemplary punishment is needed to deter these crimes 'entre estos naturales.' '(No se encuentra la defensa)' — defense not in the record. Orig. pp.417–453.

author: Comte. José Antonio Navarrete
summary: Muster — San Blas infantry company in California. Compañía de Infantería de San Blas at Monterey: Capn de Cab. grad. Navarrete (Comte.), Tte. de inf. Antonio Valle, Subtte. Francisco de Haro, 1 sargto 1º, 2 sargtos 2º, 1 tambor, 5 cabos 1º, 5 cabos 2º, 86 solds = Total 105. Arrival narrative: this veteran infantry co. (aggregated to the fija of the Apostadero de San Blas) came to Monterey in 1819 — capitán + 50 men embarked on the *San Carlos* (?) 7 Jun 1819; the Tte., Subtte. + 50 men on the frigate *Reyna de los Ángeles* same 7 Jun; *San Carlos* contingent disembarked Monterey 28 Aug, frigate's 25 Jun; by Oct the Tte. + 1 sargto + 4 cabos + 35 solds detached to presidio San Francisco. Orig. pp.458–473.

author: Comte. Navarrete
summary: San Blas infantry co. muster 1820 ('visto el extracto anterior'): baja — soldado Tomás Guillén died 14 Apr; 2 cabos + 14 solds returned to San Blas on the merchant ship *San Francisco* in Oct by gov't order. Orig. pp.474–515.

author: Comte. Luis Argüello
summary: SF artillery & ordnance census. *En la batería* [Castillo de San Joaquín]: 3 cañones de hierro de á 24, 1 de á 12, 1 de á 12 reventado (burst), 2 de á 8. *En el Presidio:* 6 medias culebrinas de á 8, 2 pedreros de bronce de á 2. Plus 'una considerable cantidad de balas de todos calibres, pólvora y utensilios.' Orig. p.576.

author: Gob. Solá
summary: Habilitado-general crisis. Solá resolves to send Capt. de la Guerra y Noriega to México to attend to the *situados* (subsidy) accounts of California & demand accounts from the interim habilitado José Antonio [Ormart?] and the current one, Alfz Gervasio Argüello; needs a monthly 100-peso assignment for the 4 presidios to cover de la Guerra's extraordinary travel costs. Notes he failed to get Gervasio Argüello to go to México to exercise the Habilitado General post — Argüello has been ~3 years stuck in Guadalajara. Orig. pp.518–521.

author: Comte. José Mª Estudillo
summary: Monterey presidial-company muster (companion to Doc 347): Tte. Estudillo, Alfz/Tte. grad. José Estrada, Cirujano Manuel Quijano, 2 sargtos, 10 cabos, 1 cadete, 66 solds = 82; + 1 sangrador, 1 carpintero, 2 herreros = 4; Artilleros 5; Inválidos (1 sargto, 8 cabos, 19 solds) = 28. Orig. pp.524–599.

author: Comte. Francisco María Ruiz
summary: San Diego presidial-company muster: 1 capn, 1 tte, 1 alfz, 2 sargtos, 7 cabos, 1 cadete, 59 solds = 72; Artilleros 3; Inválidos (4 sargtos, 15 solds) = 19. Orig. pp.605–629.

author: Comte. Francisco María Ruiz
summary: Mission/population census, SD district (españoles y otras castas + indios): Presidio SD 130 h, 117 m = 247; Mis. San Diego (2 mis.) 830 h + 595 m indios = 1425, + 12 españoles → 1437; San Luis Rey (2 mis.) 1320 h + 1265 m = 2585, + 41 españoles → 2626; San Juan Capistrano (2 mis.) 580 h + 498 m = 1078, + 63 españoles → 1141. 'á la vuelta' (continues past n327).

author: Santiago Argüello
summary: Tithe (diezmos) account SD 1820: colectado $721 + colección de individuos $173 + descuento del producto del ganado $30 = $924 total. Orig. p.7.

author: J. Joaquín Maitorena
summary: Importe of presidio existencias delivered to sargto Carlos Antonio Carrillo = $1048. Orig. pp.14–15.

author: Maitorena
summary: Orden de pago to the Admin. Gral de correos, $11.7½, net postal revenue from 1 Jan to date. Orig. p.22.

author: Maitorena
summary: Cuenta general de la Habilitación: Cargo $124,771[?]; Data $122,133[?] (incl. existencias $918). Orig. pp.28–29, 39–40.

author: Bernardo de Miramón
recipient: Comte. Gral del Estado de México
summary: List of convicts (reos) deported to California, 1821 — Iturbide-era insurgent partisans of 'el faccioso [Mariano] Gómez': Antonio Avela ('Capitán del faccioso Gómez… concurrió á varios asesinatos y salteos'); José Mª Fonseca ('pseudo secretario del vecino Mariano Gómez… de mala conducta'); Fernando Coste ('Capitán y uno de los primeros caudillos de la facción… de muy mala fama'); José Mª Gallegos ('Capitán de Gómez y reunidor y alistador de gente en los pueblos'); Joaquín Solís ('oficial agente de Gómez; muy mala conducta y ladrón') — *I'm inferring* this is likely the future leader of the 1829 Solís revolt in California; to verify; Manuel & Teodoro Gonzáles ('conocidos por los 'Pípilos'… ladrones… concurrieron al robo de los Ingleses en la Hacienda de Sta Gertrudis, camino de Perote'). Orig. pp.45–46.

author: Matías Guerrero
summary: Relación de obras nuevas y de recomposición in the Real Maestranza de Artillería, months Sept–Oct + Jun, Jul, Aug. Orig. pp.47–93.

author: Ignacio Martínez, juez fiscal
summary: Sumaria vs. German Rodríguez, soldado of the San Blas veteran co., for stoning (apedreado) Cabo 1º Manuel Alvarado on the morning of 29 May. Escribano sargto Pedro Castillo. Sentenced 4 años de presidio, obras públicas con grillete al pie. Orig. pp.99–118.

author: José de la Guerra y Noriega
summary: Hoja de servicio de Gabriel Moraga — edad 55, 37 años de servicio (de Teniente con el grado 6 [años], de Alférez 5, de Sargto 6, de soldado 4); 116 campañas[?] y 10 acciones de guerra [reading of 'campañas' uncertain — possibly 'heridas'; flag `[?]`]. Orig. p.118.

author: José Ramírez
summary: Sumaria vs. soldado de caballería of Monterey Hermenegildo Vázquez for killing/maltreating an ox (buey); left to the Superior Gobierno to set the penalty. Orig. pp.161–198.

author: Fiscal José Antonio Navarrete ("varias firmas, entre ellas la de Solá")
summary: Sumaria é información vs. Domingo Peralta, vecino of Pueblo San José, for 'mala amistad' (illicit relations) with María Petra Vázquez. Escribano José Berreyesa. Sentence: José Tiburcio[?] Peralta (father) to pay $200 to Tiburcio (the girl's grandfather) — $100 en libranza vs. the Habilitado Gral + $100 in cattle; Domingo not to impede a marriage if desired. Orig. pp.200–276.

author: Santiago Argüello, fiscal
summary: Diligencias on the bodies of soldado Pablo Sarmiento & the neófita Apolonia (mission) found dead — possible homicide. Escribano soldado J. Antonio Pico, cadete José Moraga. Neophytes Apolonio & Urbano arrested on suspicion; fiscal finds no legal proof of homicide → recommends freeing them after 13 months' prison. Orig. pp.277–351.

author: Gob. Pablo Vicente de Solá
summary: Bando licensing a tavern (taberna) at Monterey presidio — 8 conditions: cuartillo de aguardiente 6 reales, de vino 3 reales; no liquor to any Indian; sales only with the Comandante's boleta; closed on holidays, open 7am–7pm (–6pm Nov 1–Apr 30); arrivals with liquor to present boleta to the Habilitado, paying $1/barrel aguardiente, $6/barrel vino; only one tavern permitted, clandestine sale = 2 months' prison + grillete on public works; liquor seized held in deposit. Orig. pp.366–367.

author: Gob. Solá
summary: Censorship order — transcribes the Viceroy's order to prevent circulation of subversive newspapers, namely *'Español constitucional'* and *'Gabinete de curiosidades políticas y literarias de España é Indias,'* published by expatriate Spaniards [Trienio Liberal–era liberal press]. Orig. p.367.

author: Gob. Solá
summary: Anglo-American timber-ship order. Transcribes a Viceregal decree re an Anglo-American frigate (capt. 'Robert[?]') arrived at San Blas from Guayaquil intending to load California timber for the San Blas apostadero; warns the captain that otherwise his ship & cargo would be confiscated if he passed to these [Calif.] ports. Orig. p.370.

author: Fiscal Francisco de Haro; Cirujano Manuel Quijano
summary: Sumaria vs. sargto 2º of the San Blas veteran co. José Antonio Grande (ordered by Tte. Antonio del Valle). '(Parece estar incompleta esta sumaria.)' Orig. pp.373–406.

author: José Estrada
summary: Sumaria vs. soldado Eduardo Garnica & paisano Manuel Gross for wounds inflicted on the latter by the former. 'No viene ninguna sentencia.' Orig. pp.407–424.

author: Gervasio Argüello, Habilitado Gral
summary: Sum of the factura sent to the Gov. of California for troop supply = $41,317 (data igual). Orig. pp.425–432.

author: José Estrada
summary: 1821 budget (presupuesto) for the troops & officers of the presidio = $23,791. Orig. p.437.

author: José María Estudillo
summary: Hoja de servicios del Capitán Francisco María Ruiz — 40 años de servicio, edad 66; rank progression listed; 'ha hecho varias *mariscadas*[?] (correrías) por el canal y sierra de Sta Bárbara, Valle de los Tulares y concurrió á la expedición general del Río Colorado.' Orig. p.585.

author: Cabo de Artillería José Homobono Meneses (Vº Bº Estudillo)
summary: Fort Guijarros (San Diego) artillery & munitions census. 'Estado de la artillería, municiones &c. que tiene el Fuerte de Guijarros,' rated buenos/medianos/inútiles: cañones de fierro 6 (buenos), de bronce 2 buenos + 5 medianos; 995 balas de fierro; 219 saquetes de metralla; 8 arrobas cuerda mecha; cartuchos llenos de á 8 (114), á 6 (154), á 4 (42); etc. Decay note (verbatim): 'Cinco de los cañones de bronce se hallan muy desfogonados; las cureñas no sirven. Las cureñas de los de fierro se hallan podridas.' (5 bronze cannon badly vent-worn; carriages useless; iron-gun carriages rotted.) Orig. p.1 (tomo LII).

author: Manuel Gómez
summary: Maestranza de artillería: presupuesto de sueldos, 1st semester 1821 = $3207. Orig. pp.123–124.

author: Matías Guerrero
summary: Maestranza de artillería: presupuesto de sueldos, 1st semester = $3125. Orig. pp.125–126.

author: Fiscal Francisco de Haro
summary: Sumaria vs. soldados José Carlos & Estevan Higuera of this company for escaping prison & taking refuge in the church (Iglesia) [asylum]. Escribano José Ignacio Sáforo[?]. Fact confirmed; left to the Jefe Superior to sentence. Orig. pp.149–174.

author: Gob. Solá
summary: Sentences soldados Carlos & Higuera to 10 months' grillete al pie, the first reloaded with 2 extra years' service as a repeat offender for pawning/selling his kit (prendas). Orig. p.175.

author: Fiscal José Antonio Navarrete
summary: Sumaria vs. María Salomé, neófita de San Carlos, for beating Rosa and causing her death. Escribano Sebastián Rodríguez. 'No viene la sentencia.' Orig. pp.177–201.

author: Capitán/Fiscal José Antonio Navarrete
summary: Sumaria vs. Valerio, neófito de San Juan Bautista, for killing a fellow neophyte of the same mission. Escribano sargto J. Antonio Garisola[?]. Diligencias remitted to Gov. Solá to sentence. Orig. pp.302–317.

author: Fiscal Tte. Manuel Gómez
summary: Sumaria vs. soldado Simeón Cacho for disrespect to Tte. Antonio del Valle. Escribano Paulino Vitar[?]. Fiscal asks Cacho's release, the disrespect charge unproven. Orig. pp.218–257.

author: Gob. Solá; fiscal Domingo Carrillo
summary: Proceso vs. soldado of Mazatlán Jesús/José Carrejos[?] for insubordination toward Alférez Ignacio Delgado. Solá needs more on the officer's conduct (whether he kept the company's laws); names de la Guerra y Noriega to investigate. Escribano soldado José Antonio Selegano[?]. Orig. pp.358–373.

author: Fiscal Francisco de Haro
summary: Causa vs. Benhamito[?] & Simona, Christian Indians of San José, for the killing of [Santa] María, a Christian Indian of the mission — a fight in which Benhamito beat her and Simona finished the killing when he left her alone. Conflicting recommendations: Haro asks the capital penalty *not* be applied to Benhamito; Tte. Ignacio Martínez (juez fiscal) asks Benhamito be hanged and Simona 8 years' confinement. Escribano José Berreyesa. 'No viene la resolución del Gobierno.' Orig. pp.285–357.

author: Fiscal Ignacio Martínez
summary: Sumaria vs. José Castillo for estupro of his sister-in-law (cuñada) Ramona García. Fiscal asks 6 yrs' exile to SD with grillete + public works; 4 yrs' depósito for her; both fined $20[?] for her & for support of the child she bore. Orig. pp.338–349.

author: Juez José Joaquín Maitorena
summary: Diligencias vs. soldado José Ignacio Lugo (18) for attempting to rape María Josefa López, age 8, injuring her 'hasta el punto de hacerla sangre.' Sentenced 2 años de presidio con grillete al pie. Orig. pp.350–362.

author: Fiscal Maitorena
summary: Diligencias vs. militia-artillery soldado Rosalino Olivera, accused of desertion; given 8 months' presidio + public works. Orig. pp.363–381.

author: José Estrada
summary: War-council death sentence of 'el indio Pomponio.' Diligencias to investigate the killing of soldado Manuel Varela; the Consejo de Guerra condemns the Indian Pomponio to death 'por varios crímenes que ha cometido.' Signed by a council: José de la Guerra y Noriega, Luis Antonio Argüello, José Ramírez, Santiago Argüello, Ignacio Vallejo, Carlos Antonio Carrillo, José Joaquín de la Torre, José Raimundo Estrada. ⚠️ *I'm inferring* this is the noted fugitive Pomponio; the standard account dates his execution to Sept 1824 — the 1821 date here is as Savage wrote it (flag the discrepancy; do not silently reconcile). Orig. pp.382–432.

author: Gob. Solá; comisionado Capt. José Antonio Navarrete
summary: Sumaria información vs. José Antonio Robles, poblador of Branciforte, for bad conduct; the Comandante orders him banished to Los Ángeles; Robles protests by memorial. Escribano sargto J. Antonio Garisola. Orig. pp.403–506.

author: Manuel Gómez
summary: Presupuesto de sueldos y utensilios for the Real Cuerpo de Artillería, 1821 — líquido haber $19,475. Orig. pp.507–508.

author: Santiago Argüello
summary: Presupuesto del situado of the SD company, 1821 — líquido haber $19,900. Orig. p.571.

author: Fiscal cadete Domingo Carrillo
summary: Sumaria vs. soldado of Mazatlán Tiburcio Valenciano for raising his hand against cabo 2º de artillería José [Homobono] Meneses. Escribano soldado Hildefonso Velázquez. Sentence: 3 months' arrest in the Castillo for cabo Meneses(?), 6 months' public works + grillete for the soldier. Orig. pp.635–687.

author: Gob. Pablo Vicente de Solá
themes: foreign-ships; americans; contraband; smuggling; open-door; monterey
summary: Capture of two Americans from a smuggling schooner (CLOSED). Diligencias on an accusation against 'un tal Maliga[?],' by Antonio Briones, Ignacio Valencia, Mariano Mariné[?], Desiderio Olvera, Paulino Pico et al., in the apprehension of two Americans from a schooner on the coast (~Nov 7): seeing the contrabandista vessel, Briones signaled it to send a launch, which came with pilot & interpreter, bringing goods to trade; Briones — in the name of Capt. de la Guerra y Noriega — seized them and some effects. Outcome: seized goods' value assigned to repair of the pueblo church; Briones 6 months, [companion] 4 months presidio with grillete on Sta Bárbara public works; helpers 1 month arrest in the Guard. Orig. pp.698–718.

author: Santiago Argüello
summary: Declaraciones on insults (injurias) by sargto Damaso Rodríguez against soldado José Pérez, occasioned by a woman; Pérez's drunkenness led to disorders. Orig. pp.719–742.

author: Gob. Solá
summary: Sentences artillery soldado Rosalino Olivera to 2 months' grillete on public works for pawning/selling his equipment (correaje, sillas, etc.). Orig. pp.743–752.

author: José Mª Estudillo
summary: Causa vs. soldado Tomás Morales of the Mazatlán co. for getting Tiburcio Arce[?] drunk and having him whipped[?]; Solá returns the declarations as insufficient to sentence. Orig. pp.753–776.

author: Francisco Mª Ruiz
summary: Sumaria vs. soldados Joaquín Castro & Enrique[?] Espinosa for insubordination to cabo Abran Peña[?], dawn of 16 Nov 1821. Solá sentences the cabo to 15 days' arrest, the two soldiers to 6 months' labor + grillete on the new church of Los Ángeles. Orig. pp.276–296.

author: Manuel Gómez
summary: Cuenta de los haberes of the artillery troop, 1821: Data $63,817; Cargo $75,124; a favor de Gómez $8,695. Orig. pp.85–88.

author: Manuel Gómez (sin firma)
summary: Final doc. Alcance del Cuerpo de Artillería through Dec 1820 = $13,717; 'viene una lista con especificación de nombres y cantidades.' Orig. p.907.

author: Luis Antonio Argüello
summary: Estado de fuerza efectiva (veterana y milicias): artillería 8, total 127. Officers named: Tte. Ignacio Martínez, Alf. Santiago Argüello, Sgt. Luis Peralta, grad. Alf. José Sánchez, cadete Luis Ig. Estudillo, Antonio Valle

author: Paulino Vitería (pagador)
summary: Maestranza de Artillería — cuenta de caudales recibidos y gastados desde 1 Julio

author: testimony re: Ventura Pinto / Encarnación Ramírez
summary: Criminal/moral conduct case — full declarante testimony (families, house, conduct of Ventura's daughters 'muy regular')

author: Cadete Domingo Carrillo (juez), Mariano G. Vallejo (escribano)
summary: Causa criminal vs. miliciano Gracia Larios for robbing otter pelts (3 Sept); guilty but drunk → 2 yrs public works w/ grillete

author: Juan M.ª Ibarra (fiscal), Franco. de Araujo (escribano)
summary: Sumaria vs. deserters Apolinario Uribe, José M.ª Bermúdez, Antonio Alipas, Manuel Valencia (Compañía de Loreto) from Sta. Catarina detachment; left to Echeandía to sentence

author: Echeandía; Rodrigo del Pliego (investigator)
summary: Averiguación vs. Bautista Victoriano, neophyte of S. Juan Bautista, suspected of killing the adult Indian Pablo; not concluded

author: testimony cited by Rubio; witness Marta Higuera
summary: Criminal case (Rubio homicide/robbery; Olivas house, Juan García; vigilia de San Lorenzo 9 Aug) — full testimony

author: Juan M.ª Ibarra (fiscal)
summary: Sumaria vs. soldiers León Rosas & J. Cañedo for robbing cpl. Hilario García (munitions room: $11.5r, shoes, cigars, silk, 11 hides); only Rosas guilty; fiscal cites ordenanza penalty of hanging for theft inside barracks. Fin del tomo LXVII

author: Subtte. José Ramírez (habilitado)
summary: Cuenta de la partida de artillería: recibió $5,039 (Jun 1819–May 1820), distribuyó $4,708, saldo $331 entregado al Tte. Manuel Gómez

author: Tte. Manuel Gómez
summary: Cuenta de caudales, Maestranza de artillería: entradas $2,184 (Real Caja del Rosario $1,477, bergantín *Europa* $570, otra $197); pagado $2,155; existente $29

author: Gov. Pablo V. Solá; fiscal José Joaquín Maitorena
summary: Indulto: frees militiaman José M.ª Domínguez & brother Nemesio (sumariados for theft of 34 cattle; fiscal sought 4 & 8 yrs presidio) under the amnesty for the establishment of the imperial Congreso de Córtes, on condition they repay the 34 head

author: fiscal Subtte. Luis Ramírez (Cuerpo imperial de artillería)
summary: Causa criminal: soldier José Sánchez shot & killed fellow presidial soldier Luis González (night of the 23rd) after a drunken altercation (hair-pulling, scuffle, lance taken twice). Fiscal: Sánchez provoked → asks 10 yrs presidio (not death); corporal punished per R.O. 26 Sept 1776 for abandoning guard

author: fiscal Ignacio Martínez; cmdt. Capt. Luis Antonio Argüello
summary: Causa criminal: Sgt. Pedro del Castillo (San Blas veteran infantry co.) accused of insubordination to Lt. Antonio Valle (Nov 4). Confessed disrespect, blamed wine + provocation. Fiscal: reprimand, absolve, arrest-days as punishment

author: Gov. Solá
recipient: fiscal Tte. Manuel Gómez; escribano artilleryman Joaquín Piña; arrest by Alf. José Mariano Estrada / cpl. Alfonso Rodríguez
themes: criminal; incest; sexual-violence; gender; women; monterey
summary: Incest sumaria (causa por incesto en 1er grado). Father (surnamed Pinto/'Quinto') accused of repeated rape of daughters M.ª Gertrudis & M.ª Concepción (tied victim by neck with cord; threatened to kill them if they reported); allegedly brought Manuel Mendoza + an unnamed Indian to assault them to shift blame. Reported by brother-in-law Juan Germán. ~58 pp. of testimony (Gertrudis, Mendoza, Encarnación Ramírez, Gabriela Higuera, Villareal, careos). Gov. Solá's signature absent from copy

author: Cpl. Olivera
recipient: Capt. José de la Guerra (cmdt. Sta. Bárbara); sumaria by Sgt. Dámaso Rodríguez, escribano Rafael Pico; defender Alf. José Joaquín Maitorena
themes: criminal; homicide; indigenous; neophyte; mission; gender; san-buenaventura
summary: Neophyte Fabio stabbed & killed his wife Joviniana (2 neck, 1 back, 1 arm wounds). Defender pleads adultery + provocation (she attacked him w/ knife) and that Fabio is barely Christianized. Fiscal's dictamen treats minimal Christianization as mitigation — baptized 6–7 yrs, never confessed, 'sin ninguna emoción en las cosas de Nuestra Sagrada religión,' ignorant he could appeal to justice → asks 5 yrs presidio public works, removed from area (not death)

author: Gov. Solá
recipient: fiscal Subtte. Francisco de Haro; cont. by Tte. Ignacio Martínez, escribano José Fernández
summary: Desertion case vs. Francisco Bizcarra (took church sanctuary, 'tomó iglesia,' after deserting). No defense appeared. Fiscal: simple desertion (returned spontaneously within 3 days) → 4 months prison + plaza works w/ grillete, re-serve enlistment from 8 Jan

author: defender Javier Alvarado
summary: Wounding case: defends Vicente Sánchez (accused of wounding Fernández); attributes to a heated moment, asks light penalty

author: Gov. Solá orders Capt. José Antonio Navarrete; report by alcalde Francisco Castro / cmdt. Sgt. Luis Peralta
summary: San José resident Venancio Mojica gravely wounded Juan Hernández (regidor of San José) with a belduque. Mojica's plea: self-defense (Hernández beat him with a stick in his own house); fled to church sanctuary, extracted by 2nd regidor Leandro Galindo. *Savage notes the continuation is missing*

author: Cmdt. Luis Antonio Argüello
recipient: fiscal Tte. Ignacio Martínez, escribano José Fernández
themes: criminal; homicide; indigenous; children; mission-dolores; altimira
summary: Child homicide. Body of neophyte shepherd boy Juan found near Mission SF, rawhide cord at neck. Four neophyte boys — Marcelo (11), Ventura & Ildefonso (9), Vicente Francisco (10) — strangled him with cord, sand in mouth, kicking, over a quarrel about fetching tuna. Full confession; baptism records signed by Fray José Altimira

author: Cmdt. Argüello
recipient: fiscal Tte. Ignacio Martínez, escribano cabo José Reyes Berroyosa
themes: criminal; homicide; indigenous; mission; death-penalty; iturbide-empire
summary: Mission SF Indians Cosme, Felipe, Emiliano, Sempero prosecuted for killing Hermón Esteban & Fidel (asleep) night of 2 May. Original has drawings of the weapons (knife, hammer, pike). Fiscal: treacherous murders → death by hanging for Sempero, Emiliano & Adriano 'en nombre de la Soberana Junta de la Regencia del imperio mexicano'; Felipe de Venecia 10 yrs presidio + expatriation from mission, to publicly witness the others' execution

author: fiscal Tte. Ignacio Martínez, escribano cabo José Reyes Berroyosa
