summary: The fiscal's report in the Charles Laing contraband case (escribano Cadete Ignacio del Valle): Laing landed at San Quintín & the ensenada de Todos Santos, introduced effects into the territory's pueblos without paying duties or licence, \"saltando en los puntos donde no es permitido\" — convicted by his own declarations; the fiscal asks confiscation of all the goods introduced. Orig. pp.699–763.

author: Echeandía
summary: Repeats Echeandía's decree in the Laing case (already copied at pp.12–13 of this cuaderno = Doc 15). Orig. p.764.

author: Guillermo Cota, Manuel Domínguez, Fco. Morales
summary: Los Ángeles municipal revenues 1829: aguardiente sold retail $293.3.6, wine $45, judicial fines $158 (+ $96 carryover) = $592.6.3; fund on hand $150.5. (Early LA ayuntamiento finance.) Orig. pp.766–768.

author: Ignacio Martínez, Habilitado
summary: SF special revenues 1829 (tobacco, mail, iron duties) by month. Orig. pp.798–812.

author: Juan Bandini, Comisario Subalterno
themes: finance; san-diego; bandini; franklin; foreign-ship; mazatlan-squadron
summary: San Diego treasury statement, Sept 1829. In $26,631 (existence $25,342 + mission supplies + a payment re the fragata Franklin $826). Out $33,710 — incl. pay of the cavalry company $671, Mazatlán squadron $404, B. Cal. frontier support $247, artillery piquete $168, the engineer corps (Pacheco) $198, loans to Missions San Diego ($250) & San Miguel ($146). Existence $23,261. (Bandini's commissary accounts; the American Franklin in the ledger.) Orig. p.807.

author: Ignacio Martínez, Habilitado
themes: finance; san-francisco; franklin; whalers; foreign-ship; anchorage-fees
summary: San Francisco treasury statement, Dec 1829. Income $10,768 — incl. tobacco $195, mission supplies $4,543, Monterey/SD treasury transfers, a $1,000 debt to the fragata Franklin (re Mission San José powder), foreigner-import duties $177, and whaling-ship anchorage fees $87. Paid $10,444. (Foreign-ship/whaler revenue at SF.) Orig. p.811.

author: Romualdo Pacheco
themes: missions; chaplains; santa-barbara; spaniards; asia; expulsion
summary: Chaplains & secular Spaniards in the SB jurisdiction: Frs. Marcos Antonio de Vitoria (La Purísima), Blas Ordaz (Sta Inés[?]), Antonio Jayme (Sta Bárbara), Fco. Javier de Uría & Suñer (San Buenaventura), Gonzalo de Ibarra (San Fernando); seculars: 4 of the Batallón Hispano (one married to a native-born woman), 1 from the warship Asia (resident since well before), 2 invalid soldiers + Dn Miguel Calabras[?] & Dn Diego Fernández. (Spanish-expulsion-era census of resident Spaniards.) Orig. p.813.

author: José Joaquín Maitorena
summary: SB presidial company net pay 1825, $7,814. Orig. p.818.

author: Juan Mª Ibarra, fiscal
summary: Causa vs. soldier Domingo Olivas for perjury (false oath in a case against the neophyte José Fidel[?]). Fiscal: per ordinance he merits being shot, but given the trivial matter & his ignorance of what swearing means, recommends 4 months' hard labor with a shackle. Echeandía absolves him (time served); sentence executed 13 Jul. Orig. pp.1–22.

author: Echeandía
summary: Resolution of the bigamy case vs. Alférez José Mª Ramírez: accused of marrying Dolores Palomares (daughter of retired soldier José Palomares) at Los Ángeles while married in México to Dolores Olvera; separated from his wife 2 yrs 3 mo during the case. Per Ramírez's confession, Olvera had been only his concubine (family by her) not legal wife. Echeandía, finding bigamy not proven, orders him to rejoin his legal wife & notifies the ecclesiastical curia. Orig. pp.23–254.

author: Santiago Argüello, fiscal
summary: Causa vs. soldier Isaac Salgado for slapping a soldier's wife (in the house where he was visiting, not in front of Capt. Miguel González as charged); Tte. Ibarra told González the woman deserved it for receiving visits she shouldn't. Fiscal leaves the penalty to the Superioridad. Orig. pp.255–273.

author: Echeandía
themes: military-justice; santa-barbara; anti-spanish; expulsion; press; carrillo; pacheco; mexican-period
summary: Causa vs. José Antonio Noriega + Joaquín & Raimundo Carrillo. Roots in a published accusation: in El Correo de la Federación Mexicana No. 160 (9 Nov 1828), Capt. Miguel González printed a communiqué from SB alleging that 5 Spanish captains arrived hidden aboard a frigate behind the castillo and that cadets in Spanish insignia (Noriega, Raimundo & Joaquín Carrillo) signaled/boarded it past the guard — artillery officers (Rodrigo del Pliego, Mariano Vaquero) readied cannon; the \"Spanish frigate\" proved \"un puro chasco\" (a hoax) but González warned it might be a real \"intentona.\" Pacheco as fiscal finds it a youthful prank (\"á guisa de juguete\"), González's frigate/campaign claims mere supposition, and recommends leniency (no sentence). A vivid window into the 1828–29 anti-Spanish/Californio political tension (Mexican-born officer vs. native cadets). Orig. pp.274–307.

author: Santiago Argüello, fiscal
summary: Solís-revolt fallout: causa vs. artillery Sargento José Mª Mederos for alleged participation in Solís's plan (per what he told Fr. Vicente Pasqual de Oliva / Menéndez — the Monterey rising \"se había demorado… desde el mes de Julio\"). On Capt. Portilla's & Alf. Ramírez's reports, the fiscal recommends release. Escribano Cadet Ignacio del Valle. Orig. pp.521–550.

author: Guillermo Cota, Alcalde
summary: Adultery investigation of Dolores Palomares (wife of Alf. Ramírez) — opened at Ramírez's request via Fr. Oliva; found innocent, her \"illness\" explained as a gynecological condition. (Interleaved with the Ramírez bigamy sumaria, Doc 34.) Orig. pp.117–137.

author: (under Petronilo Ríos, Ayudante de la plaza — Solís-revolt period)
summary: Causa — theft of caballada (horses) implicating the American Alberto Gilbert & Alférez Gracia Sánchez (accused of complicity). Juez fiscal cabo Petronilo Ríos. Dated 21 Nov 1829 at Monterey \"Ayudante de la plaza\" — i.e., during the brief period of Solís's control of Monterey (the revolt). (Result not found.) Orig. pp.551–562.

author: Santiago Argüello
summary: SD permanent cavalry company review Jan 1829: 57 plazas; 14 invalids ($214). Orig. pp.565–567.

author: Argüello
summary: SD company net pay May 1829, $1,414. Orig. p.584.

author: Rodrigo del Pliego
summary: Defense in the causa vs. cabo Bernardo Coril (accused of slapping his Sargento José Mederos). Orig. pp.440–454.

author: Mariano Elizalde[?], Juez Fiscal
summary: Causa \"por adhesión á España\" vs. Don Manuel Herrera (b. Tepic, age 55): accused of telling the ayuntamiento \"¿por qué no se pronunciaba por España?\" and of not even raising the national flag on the 16th [Sept]. Fiscal: impose the merited penalty. An expulsion-era political-loyalty case. Orig. pp.455–476.

author: Ignacio Martínez
summary: SF company Jan 1829: 60 plazas. Orig. pp.480–484.

author: Lázaro Piña (rev. Ignacio Martínez)
summary: SF artillery & ordnance inventory, July 1829: bronze cannon various calibers 7 serviceable + 1 useless; iron cannon 5 serviceable + 5 useless; plaza guns ~3 (2 serviceable); 4 marine carriages; loading tools (atacadores, cucharas, sacatrapos…); 1,225 round shot, 525 tin-canister grapeshot; powder, parque supplies. (Mexican-period SF battery state — for the artillery register.) Orig. pp.495–497.

author: José Palomares, sargento retirado, fiscal
summary: Causa vs. artillery militiaman Juan de Murillo for wounding José Antonio Niño — found to have acted in self-defense (Niño threatened him with a navaja). Penalty left to the Superioridad. Orig. pp.498–519.

author: Ignacio Martínez, fiscal
themes: indigenous-violence; punitive-expedition; san-jose; santa-clara; vallejo; estanislao-revolt; military-justice; gender
summary: Inquiry into Indians of San José & Santa Clara killed on a punitive expedition. A party under Alférez [Gonzalo?] de Vallejo ( the first name as written resembles \"Gonzalo,\" but in context — SF company alférez 1º, 1829 — this is almost certainly Mariano Guadalupe Vallejo) pursuing Christian Indians of San José & Santa Clara killed & robbed Indians incl. 3 defenseless old women; the fiscal finds only one Indian was shot (not lanced) and that a Monterey soldier ([Joaquín?] Alvarado) killed an elderly gentile woman — \"aunque fuese en el acto de la guerra, no le es permitido á ninguna persona [atacar] á los del sexo femenino\" — recommending 5 extra years on the Baja Cal. frontier. I'm inferring this is the atrocity inquiry following M. G. Vallejo's May–June 1829 campaign against Estanislao (an SJ-mission neophyte) — dates/place/perpetrator fit, but verify against the Estanislao record before asserting. Orig. pp.518–541.

author: (unsigned)
summary: Extract of the sumaria vs. artilleryman Manuel Rojas for words against the officers (cf. Doc 22) — suspended as Rojas contradicted himself; the fiscal asks no extraordinary punishment, he being new to arms. Orig. pp.542–545.

author: Romualdo Pacheco
summary: SB company Nov 1827: 64 plazas + 8 artillerymen. Orig. p.558.

author: (notario)
summary: Causa vs. artilleryman Domingo Lagunas for wounding another — \"no presenta interés.\" Orig. pp.560–569.

author: José Fernández del Campo
summary: Monthly budget of the permanent artillery (55 plazas), $585. Orig. p.591.

author: Juan José Rocha
summary: The San Blas permanent infantry company had 57 plazas at the comisario's review. Orig. pp.612–613.

author: Rocha
summary: San Blas company net pay this month, $455. Orig. p.614.

author: Romualdo Pacheco
summary: SB company July 1827: 65 plazas (escolta/correos/comisiones), only 22 present for service; one discharged \"por ser español\" (Spanish-expulsion). Orig. p.670.

author: (unsigned; Echeandía / Jefatura Política)
themes: finance; alta-california; presidio-decay; fiscal-collapse; virmond; mexican-period
summary: Treasury statement of the Territory of Alta California, 1828. Income $24,503 — incl. commercial-ship duties (intervención $2,307, tonnage $2,116, anchorage, avería) ≈ $4,700, missions $6,570, individual loans $695, draughts on the Comisaría de Occidente $5,205, tobacco $1,422. Expenses $31,584 — military payroll dominant (the 2 permanent cavalry companies $14,935, engineer dept $1,001, San Blas troops $1,571, Loreto frontier detachment $1,757, Mazatlán squadron $2,674, Jefe Político's pay $1,572). Deficit $6,881. Explanatory notes: the finances fell on the Jefatura after Subcomisario José Mª Herrera was suspended (26 Oct 1827); figures compiled from the 4 presidios' habilitaduría monthly statements; \"la partida de préstamos… son el importe de los víveres… con que diariamente se socorren las tropas… de algunos años á esta parte\" — the troops supplied daily on credit for years for lack of pay; per the Comandancia's military records the payroll = $49,564 and the total Hacienda cost for the Territory (incl. Loreto/Baja) approaches $128,000/yr; merchant Enrique [Henry] Virmond named as a service provider. Orig. pp.671–676.

author: José Joaquín de la Torre, Cmdt. Militar
summary: Causa from Branciforte: a complaint that the alcalde had called a resident \"ladrón\"; the fiscal finds it untrue & the alcalde already punished by a fine; recommends punishing Jorge Salazar for refusing to tell the truth in his depositions. Orig. pp.677–700.

author: Vicente Pliego[?]
summary: SF artillery detachment review: 19 plazas. Orig. pp.712–713.

author: José Fernández del Campo
summary: The 1ª Brigada de Artillería Permanente at the comisario's review: 67 artilleros. Orig. pp.717–718.

author: Santiago Argüello
summary: SD company: 56 plazas + 20 vacancies. Orig. pp.747–750.

author: Argüello
summary: SD company net pay this month, $1,429. Orig. p.751.

author: Argüello
summary: Pay of the invalids attached to the SD company, $216. Orig. p.753.

author: (unsigned)
summary: Service record of Sargento Ignacio Vallejo (father of M. G. Vallejo): 18 yrs as soldier + 24 yrs as sergeant = 42½ yrs, entering the service 8 June 1787; campaigns vs. Indians; commissioned to the pueblo & Villa de Branciforte; swore the Independence 11 Apr 1822. (Cross-links his enlistment in [[provincial-state-papers-benicia-military-ca15]] Doc 289 — which dated the entry 1 Jul 1787; minor date discrepancy.) Orig. pp.755–756.

author: Ignacio Martínez, Habilitado
summary: SF presidial company Dec 1829: Capt. Luis Antonio Argüello; Tte. Martínez; Alférez 1º M. G. Vallejo (detached at Monterey); 2º vacant; 2 sargtos, 4 cabos, 50 soldados = 61 present; invalids + pay. Orig. pp.3–49.

author: Ignacio Martínez (Cmdt./administrador de correos/subcomisario)
summary: SF presidial company 1830 (Alférez M. G. Vallejo at Monterey; strength 51→40 across the year). Memorandum: \"En oficio de 25 de Junio de 1830 se pidió al Comandante de San Francisco una lista nominal con expresión de la fecha en que abandonaron su puesto y se presentaron los que siguieron la causa de Solís\" — a request for a named list (with dates) of the SF soldiers who joined Solís's cause (the Nov-1829 revolt). Orig. pp.5–131 (scattered).

author: Rodrigo del Pliego, Comandante
summary: Monterey presidial company 1830: Alférez Pliego; 3 sargtos, 1 cabo, 31 soldados = 36 (down from ~75 with 2 alféreces/10 cabos/61 soldados early in the year); reviewed before Manuel Jimeno Casarín (comisario subalterno) & Antonio Mª Osio (contador). Orig. pp.135–158.

author: Secretario del Gobierno [Zamorano?]
summary: Notice on land (terrenos): individuals occupying sites at the pueblo & jurisdiction of Monterey by concession of the territory's governors to apply (in person or by proxy) to the Secretaría of the Jefatura Política re their petitions for ownership. (Early Mexican-period land-grant administration.) Orig. p.161.

author: Secretario [Zamorano?] (reglamento by Echeandía, 18 Aug 1830)
themes: land-grants; livestock; brands; monterey; rancho-economy; mexican-period
summary: Cattle-roundup & land/livestock census order for the Monterey jurisdiction: holders of land (by governors' concession) & cattle to present at the rodeo from Mon 23 Aug for the Comisionado Cadete Raymundo Estrada + 2 associates to count & record (rancho name, owner, head of cattle/horses, brand & mark per the bando of 7 Oct 1827), forming a general report to the Secretaría. (Rancho/brand census — useful for californio rancho economy.) Orig. pp.161–163.

author: Secretario [Zamorano?]
summary: The same land/cattle census notice (cf. Doc 67) now for the holders of San José de Guadalupe; Comisionado Cadet Raymundo Estrada, associates Sebastián Rodríguez & Inocente García. Orig. p.164.

author: José Mª de Echeandía
summary: Echeandía's secularization bando (Jan 1831). Encloses the reglamento for the handover of the missions' temporalities to Administrators, per the 13 Sep 1813 [Cortes] law, to be circulated to the Padres Ministros; the diocesan to convert the missions into curatos/parishes with canonically-provided párrocos (or leave them to the Religiosos administering them); copies to the federal Supremo Gobierno. (Borrador.) The famous 6 Jan 1831 Echeandía secularization decree. Orig. p.165.

author: Echeandía
themes: bodega; russian-california; fort-ross; san-rafael; santa-rosa; foreign-threat; open-door; colonization; echeandia; 1831
summary: Measure to check the Russians of Ross/Bodega — the grant of \"Santa Rosa\" as a barrier. Echeandía, holding it \"de suma importancia á la seguridad é integridad del territorio nacional,\" resolves to plant a settlement on the land between Mission San Rafael and the Russian establishment of the puerto de Bodega, \"que sirva de barrera\" against foreign incursion \"para posesionarse de él, como lo han hecho del que ocupan\" (i.e., as the Russians did at Bodega) — prompted by the San Rafael missionary's fear (after a Russian reconnaissance the prior year) and to draw in the gentile Indians. He grants the tract known as Santa Rosa to the petitioner D. R. Gómez[?] (with powers for agriculture & stock-raising) under Art. 12 of the 18 Aug 1824 colonization decree, provisional pending the Diputación. (Borrador.) The Mexican continuation of Solá's 1818 San Rafael barrier strategy vs. Fort Ross. Orig. pp.166–167.

author: Alférez J. Fernández del Campo, Cmdt. Principal de Artillería
summary: Monterey artillery inventory, Mar 1830: bronze 8-pdrs 3 serviceable + 2 useless, 1×6, 1×4; iron 1×8, 6×6 (3 fit), 2×4, 1×1½; + tools, powder, balls (same per the 30 Jul extract signed by Petronilo Ríos). Orig. pp.171–179.

author: Fernández del Campo
summary: Monterey artillery detachment 1831: cabos Petronilo Ríos, Bernardo Curiel, Joaquín Piña, Octaviano Gutiérrez + 11 soldiers; \"el vestuario en su mayor parte inútil.\" Orig. p.175.

author: José Fernández del Campo, Alférez y Comandante principal
summary: 5ª Compañía de Artillería annual return, 1830: stationed at Monterey 1 cabo/1 tambor/18 soldados; San Francisco 2 cabos/6 soldados; Santa Bárbara Sargto 2º Vicente Ríos/3 cabos/6 soldados; San Diego Sargto 2º José Mariano [&] Andrés Cervantes/9 soldados. Total 1 oficial, 3 sargtos, 1 tambor, 6 cabos, 39 soldados = 49 hombres (began the year with ~60, declining). Reviewed before comisario Manuel Jimeno Casarín or contador Antonio Mª Osio. Orig. pp.179, 332 (monthly extracts scattered pp.184–216).

author: Santiago Argüello, Comandante
summary: SD presidial company May 1830: 1 teniente, 1 alférez, 3 sargtos, 5 cabos, 64 soldados + ½-pay; review detail. Orig. pp.225–226.

author: Alférez Rodrigo del Pliego
summary: Held under his command a piquete of the San Diego company = 17 soldados (detached at Monterey/interior). Orig. pp.231–235, 312, 319.

author: Ignacio Martínez, Comandante
summary: SF presidial company Mar 1831: Tte. Martínez; alférez y habilitado M. G. Vallejo; sargto graduado de alférez José Lino Sánchez; sargto 1º Nasario Berreyesa; 4 cabos, 36 soldados = 44 hombres; paid review before Antonio Mª Osio (encargado de la Hacienda del puerto de SF); artillería detachment ~6 hombres. Orig. pp.252–255.

author: Juan Bandini, Encargado de la Aduana Marítima y Comisaría Subalterna del Puerto
summary: \"Sobre sus facultades\" — replies to the Comandante Militar of SD that, regarding the demanded payments, he cannot deliberate without prior mandate of the Encargado of the territory's principal Comisaría, and will not make the payments until an order comes through the proper channel. (Bandini defining his fiscal/jurisdictional limits.) Orig. pp.260–261.

author: José de Jesús Vallejo
summary: Artillería de milicia: cabo José de Jesús Vallejo (M. G. Vallejo's brother) y Quintín Ortega = 12 soldados. Orig. p.265.

author: [outgoing artillery sargto]
summary: SF cannon-handover inventory, Mar 1830: \"Entrego hoy al Cabo Lázaro Piña: Cañones de bronce: 5 de á 1[1?] de servicio, 1 de á 8 id, 1 de á 2½ id y 1 inútil. Cañones de hierro: 3 de á 24 de servicio, 2 de á 12 inútiles, 1 de á 8 de servicio y 1 inútil, 1 de á 6 id\" + utensilios y pertrechos. (Castillo de San Joaquín / SF battery ordnance hand-off; the bronze \"de á 11\" caliber is implausible — likely \"de á 1,\" flagged.) Orig. pp.266–268.

author: Echeandía, Comandante General (from Romualdo Pacheco's hoja de servicios)
themes: solis-revolt; military-history; santa-barbara; monterey; san-francisco; pacheco; 1829
summary: Pacheco's role suppressing the Solís revolt (verbatim from his service record): \"En 3 de Dic. de 1829, siendo comandante militar accidental de Santa Bárbara, fué aprehendido por la tropa de la guarnición, sublevada de acuerdo con la de Monterrey, pretendiendo destruir… el gobierno del territorio; y en tales circunstancias, á su actividad y decisión se debió con riesgo de su vida la restauración al 2.º día de la citada plaza de Santa Bárbara; y después, mandando en jefe la división de operaciones contra los [facciosos], se logró la restauración de las plazas de Monterey y San Francisco, con lo cual volvió el territorio á su tranquilidad y orden.\" (The clearest narrative of the Solís revolt's military suppression — cf. Docs 10, 20, 37, 64.) Orig. p.270.

author: Cabo Rafael Soto — Milicia de Artillería, Destacamento de San Francisco
summary: Cabo Rafael Soto & Andrés Martínez y 18 soldados; \"esta gente estaba de servicio.\" Orig. p.275.

author: Mariano Soberanes, Alcalde / Juez fiscal
summary: Causa criminal: Alcalde Soberanes instructed a sumaria for cattle-theft — 4 reses killed (tracks found by Luis [G.?], the carcasses buried in the woods near his house) — examining Andrés Escamilla and the militiaman Antonio Aguilar. Closing note: \"Devolvieron parte del robo por conducto del Alcalde, y se castigaron gubernativamente.\" Orig. pp.281–291.

author: Ignacio Martínez, Comandante
themes: punitive-expedition; tulares; san-jose; santa-clara; estanislao; indigenous-violence; mexican-period
summary: Forwards the diligencias practiced \"en la campaña de los ríos de los Tulares con los Indios cristianos de San José y Santa Clara\" — a punitive campaign in the Tulares vs. Christian (mission-fugitive) Indians of SJ & SC. \"(No se encuentra el documento.)\" — Savage notes the underlying document is missing. (Ties to the Estanislao/Tulares thread; cf. Doc 47.) Orig. pp.292–293.

author: Domingo Carrillo, Juez fiscal
themes: military-justice; gender; poisoning[?]; adultery; santa-barbara; mexican-period
summary: Causa criminal: an averiguación opened on Vicente Ríos's complaint that the soldier [J.] Becerra had handed Ríos's wife \"unos papeles con polvos p[ar]a que lo echase en su alimento\" (powders to put in Ríos's food) and boasted that the better clothes she wore he had given her. Escribano Sargto Dámaso[?] Rodríguez. Fiscal's dictamen: per the wife's declaration Becerra's conversations with her \"pasaron á solas\"; to avert \"alg[un]a desgracia,\" Becerra to be sent back to his company and held under arrest pending the Comandante General. \"Así se hizo.\" Orig. pp.294–302.

author: Sargto 1º Romualdo[?]
summary: SB artillery handover inventory: bronze 1×6, 3×4, 1×3, 1×2 (serviceable); iron 3×4 (useless); + utensilios y pertrechos. Orig. pp.307–309.

author: Santiago Argüello, Comandante y habilitado
summary: SD presidial company annual return 1830: Capt. José Mª Estudillo (reviewed the first 4 months \"aunque siempre ausente\"); Tte. Santiago Argüello; Alf. Gervasio Argüello (absent, his son acting as habilitado); Alf. Domingo Carrillo (attached Jan); 3 sargtos, 5 cabos, 149 soldados; present strength Jan–Mar 58, Apr–Jun 54, rest of year 53; invalids under Capt. José Mª Ruiz + sargto Juan Mariner. Reviews passed before José Antonio Estudillo (\"se titulaba Comisario Subalterno Provisional\") then Juan Bandini (interino). Orig. pp.310–395.

author: Rodrigo del Pliego — hoja de servicios
themes: military-history; indigenous-violence; valerio; san-fernando; chumash; santa-barbara; solis-revolt; pliego; prosopography; mexican-period
summary: Pliego's service record. Began as alférez in Cal. 21 Dec 1824 (Monterey company → SB Aug 1827–Feb 1828); prior México service in the Regimiento de Dragones de Tlalnepantla, retired 10 Dec 1821 as Alf. de Urbanos. Notable commissions: (1) 21 July 1828 — commissioned by the SB Cmdt. with 20 men \"p[ar]a la pacificación de los indios de la Misión de San Fernando,\" captured the cabecillas and brought them prisoner to SB; (2) 19 Jan 1828 — sent with 2 soldiers to the Sierra de Najalayegua[?] \"p[ar]a perseguir al indio Valerio, que hizo temer por sus asesinatos y robos, y logró cogerlo herido de un balazo, de cuyas resultas murió luego\" — the pursuit & killing of the fugitive Indian Valerio ( almost certainly the noted fugitive Chumash neophyte Valerio of the SB back-country — dates his death to early 1828; verify against the SB-mission/Chumash record); (3) the San Blas veteran-infantry piquete July 1826–May 1827; (4) [n77, cited pp.399–400] 1 Jan–14 Apr 1830 — \"estuvo mandando un piquete de la Comp.a de Caball.a permanente del presidio de San Diego de 18 hombres… p[ar]a la persecución de Solís y cómplices\" by order of the Cmdt. Gen. of the Territory (a Solís-revolt suppression command; the SD-cavalry detail is unusual for an artillery officer — the \"Y día…\" continuation reads as Pliego's but the page jump to 399–400 leaves a slight chance it is a separate fragment). Orig. pp. (svc record body ~pp.310+; Solís entry pp.399–400).

author: Domingo Carrillo, Habilitado
summary: SB presidial company 1830: Capt. José de la Guerra y Noriega; Tte. José Joaquín Maitorena; Alf. Carrillo; sargtos Anastasio Carrillo, Dámaso[?] Rodríguez, Juan Salazar; ~6 cabos, 43 soldados (Sept–Nov); invalids 3 sargtos/1 cabo/18–19 soldados; company haber + gratificación this year $5,023, invalids $744; cost for 4 months (Aug–Nov) $5,767. Orig. pp.407–441.

author: Alférez 1º Anastasio Carrillo, Administrador de Correos
summary: Brief index/cross-ref entry noting Anastasio Carrillo as SB postmaster, with scattered page references. Orig. pp.412–3, 419, 421, 425–6, 432, 449[?].

author: Romualdo Pacheco, Comandante
themes: solis-revolt; military-history; san-francisco; mutiny; artillery; russian-frigate; argüello; de-haro; piña; 1829
summary: \"Sublevación de Solís\" — the Cabo Joaquín Piña salute causa. Pacheco orders Ramírez to open a sumaria on a complaint (relayed via art. cmdt. José Fernández del Campo from SF cmdt. Ignacio Martínez, dated 20 Feb) against artillery Cabo José Joaquín Piña. Martínez's account: on 28 Nov [1829] at 1 PM, while he was at home with his family (alférez Francisco de Haro present), Piña entered and demanded \"con mucha altanería y falta de respeto\" five rounds \"para saludar á la expedición\" [of Solís]; despite Martínez insisting only the 5 tiros (cal. 3) be taken, Piña and artillerymen Maldonado & [Pedro/Lucas] Soto hauled off a whole cajón of ammunition to the artillery cuartel and, \"poco después comenzaron á hacer el saludo al entrar la expedición de Solís, habiendo izado la bandera nacional al verificarlo.\" Witness Eusebio Soto: they took the cajón \"con el fin de hacer salva á la exp[edició]n de Solís que iba entrando,\" and Piña said \"que él quería ir con todos los artilleros á correr la misma suerte que su Comand.te y su hermano, que sabía se hallaban presos en Monterey.\" Escribanos cadet Raymundo Estrada then Sebastián[?] Rodríguez. Joaquín Piña's own deposition (n81–85) adds: a Russian frigate lay in the bay (Martínez was aboard it on 28 Nov); Solís's salute protocol was 5 shots on entry, 5 when the plan was read, 5 when the troops withdrew to quarters; the SF plaza was effectively handed over to Solís (\"antes de entregar la plaza\"; the courier José Sánchez carried Martínez's surrender-reply); and Martínez, the late ex-Gov. Luis de Argüello, alf. Sánchez & Haro were seen reading & praising Solís's plan (\"muy lindo y muy bueno… como los mandamientos de la ley de Dios\"). Piña refused to muster, retorting he \"no era indio de conquista.\" Savage notes the rest of the causa is not present and that Piña was commanding the SB artillery detachment 31 Dec 1830. → verbatim depositions in Expanded Entry A below. Orig. pp.452–475, 476.

author: Rodrigo del Pliego, Comandante
themes: rosters; monterey; solis-revolt; deportation; vallejo; mexican-period
summary: Monterey presidial company 1830: Jefe Pliego; Alf. Mariano G. Vallejo; sargto Ignacio Vallejo (enfermo); 4 cabos, 33 soldados = 40 hombres. The soldiers Pablo Vejar, Gonzalo & Victorino Altamirano, Leonardo Acedo[?], and Raymundo de la Torre were sent prisoner to San Blas \"como cabecillas en lo de Solís en la sublevación de 1829\" (named ringleaders of the Solís revolt deported). Reviewed before Manuel Jimeno Casarín (interim comisario subalterno); Feb/Mar extracts list a 2nd sargto Sebastián Rodríguez, escribano Raymundo Carrillo, 10 cabos, 58–60 soldados; pay Feb $1,461, Mar $1,389. Orig. pp.485–502.

author: Echeandía, Cmdt. Gen.
themes: military-justice; san-diego; fandango; carrillo; marrón; pío-pico; diputación; mexican-period
summary: Orders a reserved sumaria into Alférez Domingo Carrillo's scandalous conduct one night at a fandango at the civilian Anastasio Leiva's house, where soldiers of his company had mixed in; Carrillo tried to march Cabo Juan Mª Marrón back to quarters, words passed, and the officer \"le prometió su venganza.\" SD cmdt. reported it 5 Nov; Tte. Santiago Argüello opened the sumaria 10 Sept, escribano cadet Ignacio del Valle. Marrón's deposition: at ~11 PM, missing soldiers Ramón Sotelo & Esteban Higuera, he found them \"enfandangados\" at Leiva's; Carrillo, \"un poco trastornado de la cabeza\" from aguardiente, fronted him — \"Sí, sí, váyase pronto, porque si no, lo he de correr á palos\" — and drew his sword. Pío Pico (declaring himself \"vocal de la Diputación de este territorio\") testifies he was at the fandango: Marrón challenged him \"¿eres guapo ó eres mal criado?,\" Pico drew and struck him, they grappled, Pico knocked him down with \"dos cintarazos,\" then had Anastasio Machado return the cabo's sword. Other depositions (Javier Higuera, Sotelo, E. Higuera, Luis Morales) split between confirming and exculpating Carrillo. \"(No se encuentra aquí más de esta sumaria.)\" Orig. pp.504–528.

author: (Savage, in English)
summary: Brief note: \"The question of Church Asylum or protection to criminals is fully discussed\" — clarifying which criminals were / were not entitled to ecclesiastical sanctuary, and under whose authority it could be declared null; cross-ref to \"pp.42 & several subsequent ones.\" (Savage's index pointer to the Herrera asylum causa, Doc 94 / Expanded Entry B.) Orig. pp.42+.

author: Ignacio del Valle, Ayudante de Plaza, Juez fiscal
themes: ecclesiastical-asylum; church-state; military-justice; san-diego; secularization-era; herrera; gómez; josé-sánchez; mexican-period
summary: The Ignacio Leonardo Herrera ecclesiastical-asylum causa (the concrete case behind Doc 93). The civilian Ignacio Leonardo Herrera (age 29, native of Guadalajara; escribano + \"principios de flebotomía\") knifed the cavalry-sentinel Miguel Castro (cuts to arm/finger/back) on 17 May 1830, in revenge after Castro drove him \"á cintarazos\" from the calabozo (where he'd gone to give prisoner Hilario García tobacco) and mocked him; Herrera pleaded he acted \"ciego de cólera.\" He then fled the jail in his shackles and took refuge in the Mission San Diego church, claiming ecclesiastical immunity (asilo/retraimiento) — touching off a long church-vs-state battle. Fiscal Ignacio del Valle extracted him under a \"papel de iglesia\" (caución juratoria) citing the order of 7 Oct 1775; Fr. Vicente Pasqual de Oliva (SD) and Father-President Fr. José Sánchez (San Gabriel) defended the asylum (Gregorian bull Cum alias nonnulli; Benedict XIII/XIV; the 1814 Francisco de Asís precedent), while Asesor Lic. [Rafael] Gómez ruled the crime excepted (alevosía; the bull \"no está recibida\" in Spain; R. Órdenes 1750/1775/1787/1794). Outcome: Herrera sentenced to 8 años de presidio (Gómez, San Carlos 6 Nov; Echeandía decreed 22 Nov; finalized 19 Jan 1831); SD cmdt. Santiago Argüello protested Herrera was \"sentenciado sin oír el alegato de su defensor.\" → verbatim in Expanded Entry B. This Herrera (civilian scribe, b. Guadalajara) is distinct from subcomisario José Mª Herrera (the Solís ally). Orig. pp.535–645.

author: Echeandía
recipient: Cmdt. de S. Diego (fiscal Santiago Argüello)
themes: indigenous-resistance; mission-labor; vagrancy-law; horse-theft; san-diego; neophytes; mexican-period
summary: Causa vs. two neophytes Pedro & Feliciano (sent from Tecate[?] by Cabo Juan Mª Marrón) for horse-theft and because they \"no querían sujetarse á los trabajos de comunidad de su misión\" (refusing mission community labor). Argüello — here styling himself Cmdt. Militar of SD \"y encargado de los asuntos políticos de este Departamento\" — finds them liable under the vagrancy decree of 8 Mar 1828 and the supreme decree of 7 Sep 1829 (art.1 §6) on ladrones cuatreros. Echeandía's sentence (apparently in his own hand): Pedro → 2 yrs public works at the SF presidio; Feliciano → 1 yr at Monterey. Orig. pp.647–654.

author: Echeandía
themes: pasquines; anti-echeandía; monterey; josé-castro; j-b-alvarado; californio-identity; political; mexican-period
summary: Causa \"sobre pasquines contra Echeandía.\" Placards reading \"Muera Echandía\" (sic) \"en letras grandes imitando la de imprenta\" appeared at Monterey on 6–7 Dec 1830 in three spots — at Echeandía's house, on the bridge between Mariano Estrada's and Juan [Rogers] Cooper's houses, and at Esteban Munrás's house. Soldier Juan José Navarro (Mazatlán squadron) saw two muffled figures near Echeandía's door at ~2 AM, one seemingly José Castro \"el mozo\"; Echeandía suspected Castro, motive \"odiosidad á los Mexicanos del Centro.\" The fiscal swept up the young Californio elite: José Castro deposed as \"secretario del Ilustre Ayuntamiento de Monterey,\" and Juan Bautista Alvarado as \"secretario de la Diputación del Territorio\" (both said they were together at Castro's quarters that night; neither knew the author). Other witnesses: Joaquín Ortega, Joaquín Gómez, Joaquín de la Torre, José Amesti (Munrás's administrator), illiterate soldier Emeterio Espinosa. No author found — \"(Aquí cesa el procedimiento.)\" Escribano cabo Pedro Guerrero. An eve-of-1831 anti-Echeandía / Californio-vs-\"Mexicanos del Centro\" episode featuring the future 1836-movement leaders (Castro, Alvarado) as young men. scan n122 is a retake of printed p.121 (the scan index runs ahead of the printed page from n123 on). Orig. pp.759–782.

author: Ignacio Martínez
summary: Prisoner return: held in this presidio 1 soldier + 1 neófito. Orig. p.3.

author: Juan Salazar
summary: Prisoner return: held in this presidio 12 neófitos prisoners. Orig. p.4.

author: Romualdo Pacheco
summary: Prisoner return: held in this presidio 16 prisoners, \"entre neófitos y gente de razón.\" Orig. pp.5–6. scan n128 is a retake of printed p.126.

author: Rodrigo del Pliego
summary: Prisoner return: 21 de razón, 8 indios, 4 gentiles. Orig. pp.8–9.

author: Romualdo Pacheco
summary: 30 prisoners sent from SB to Monterey. Orig. pp.10–11.

author: Vicente Ríos
summary: SB ordnance: 5 good bronze cannon (various calibers), 3 useless iron, battle & marine gear, shot/cartridges/powder. Orig. pp.13–14.

author: José Mª Mederos
summary: 11 artillerymen detached at this presidio. Orig. pp.21–22.

author: Petronilo Ríos
summary: SF artillery detachment = 7 plazas. Orig. p.23.

author: José Mª Mederos
summary: SD artillery detachment pay, May 1830. Orig. p.27.

author: José Fernández del Campo
summary: The 1ª Brigada de Artillería Permanente, 5ª Comp.a, mustered 48 plazas across the 4 presidios. Orig. pp.32–37.

author: Santiago Argüello
summary: Value of supplies furnished to the national brig María Ester for the prisoners it carries = $448 (copy by Zamorano). Orig. p.40.

author: Alférez Roberto Pardo
summary: SB company muster: 37 plazas (+ invalids). Orig. pp.42–44.

author: (unsigned)
summary: Monterey company net pay this month, $666. Orig. p.45.

author: Echeandía
summary: A cuaderno of retirements & absolute discharges (retiros y licencias absolutas) granted for Alta California by Echeandía. (Prosopographical/genealogical source.) Orig. pp.46–67.

author: (various)
summary: A large block of filiaciones (enlistment/service papers), some printed, some manuscript — soldiers' personal-data records. (Prosopographical/genealogical source.) Orig. pp.68–179.

author: Romualdo Pacheco
summary: SB cavalry company 52 plazas (incl. one sick and one discharged \"por ser español\" — Spanish expulsion) + 13 men of the Acapulco infantry. Orig. p.180.

author: Santiago Argüello
summary: 12 prisoners held at this presidio. Orig. p.186.

author: Argüello
summary: SD garrison effective force: 11 artillerymen, 53 cavalry plazas, 6 of the Mazatlán [squadron]. Orig. p.197.

author: Lic. [Rafael] Gómez, Asesor del Territorio
themes: indigenous-violence; neophyte-homicide; santa-barbara; military-justice; gómez; mexican-period
summary: Criminal vs. the Santa Bárbara neophyte Telapie[?] for killing another SB-mission neophyte, Gonzalo. Fiscal Juan Salazar sought the death penalty (ordenanzas art.61 tít.10 trat.8); Asesor Gómez found the case procedurally deficient (no facultativo's wound report, no body examination, faulty depositions) and recommended 10 years public works; Echeandía agreed (counted from imprisonment, sent to SD presidio). (Orig. pp.256–77 blank but foliated.) Orig. pp.205–277.

author: José Joaquín de la Torre, fiscal
summary: Sumaria vs. Mission Soledad neophytes [Cojidos?] & Venancio for killing the Indian Anacleto (+ \"amancebamiento\" / concubinage); the accused \"tomó iglesia\" (church refuge — another asylum instance). Conclusion not present. Orig. pp.278–295.

author: Tesorero Tapia
summary: LA fondo de propios y arbitrios for the year: in $552, out $429, balance $103. dated \"Sbre 31\" (Sept has 30 days; prob. year-end Dec 31). Orig. p.296.

author: Tomás Morales
summary: $61 delivered to the ayuntamiento síndico (from the municipal fund, Jan–Apr). \"Sbre 31\" [sic]. Orig. p.297.

author: Francisco Javier Alvarado
summary: LA municipal revenue (aguardiente rent + other) for the year: $315. \"Sbre 31\" [sic]. Orig. pp.298–299.

author: Alvarado
summary: LA fines collected during the year: $155. \"Sbre 31\" [sic]. Orig. pp.300–301.

author: José Palomares
summary: Receipt for $240 salary as sub[alterno?] of his office. \"Sbre 31\" [sic]. Orig. p.304.

author: Joaquín Botiller & Luciano Valdés
summary: Two receipts ($61½ and $15½) as maestros de escuela (LA schoolteachers); Savage notes the dates the sums correspond to aren't stated. Orig. pp.305–306.

author: Alberto Gilbert (American)
themes: american; foreign-resident; horse-theft; monterey; san-juan-bautista; mexican-period
summary: \"Queja por caballada embargada.\" Gilbert's complaint over his embargoed horse-herd: a buyer (\"Colt[?]\") bought ~400 head, was intercepted near San Juan Bautista; Romero, Tomás Linares, Sergio Escamilla merged their horses in; Gilbert's money vanished; Alf. José Fernández del Campo seized men/horses; some claimed the stock was stolen; Soto et al. embargoed Gilbert's marked herd. Fernández del Campo accuses Gilbert of buying stolen horses & funding their removal by San José people; Gilbert demands proof. Fiscal Rodrigo del Pliego, escribano Zamorano; no conclusion. (Cf. Doc 39, the 1829 Gilbert horse-theft causa.) Orig. pp.308–331.

author: Lic. [Rafael] Gómez, Asesor (fiscal cadet Ignacio del Valle)
themes: indigenous-violence; mission-labor; flogging; santa-isabel; san-diego; bandini; gómez; victoria; mexican-period
summary: Proceso vs. the mayordomo García of the Estancia de Santa Isabel (SD) for cruelly flogging SD neophytes & gentiles caught for cattle-theft — 7 Indians incl. an old captain of the Ranchería de los Cogriñes[?] flogged to death (\"se acabaron 3 rollos de varas,\" García tore out the captain's hair). Fiscal del Valle sought 10 yrs presidio, condemning García (an ex-cabo who knew the rules) for punishing \"arbitrariamente.\" Defender Juan Bandini (Diputación vocal/interim SD asesor) defended routine flogging — \"tan común en toda la Cal.,\" \"el único medio q[u]e hay para contenerlos en los robos\" — and argued the captain died of a hunger strike, not the beating. Asesor Gómez: 5 yrs presidio; Gov. Victoria confirmed 20 Apr 1831. → verbatim in Expanded Entry C. Orig. pp.332–510.

author: Bernardo Curiel
summary: Permanent artillery, 5ª Comp.a: oficial+empleados 2, 1 sargto, 1 tambor, 4 cabos, 36 artilleros = 45. Savage: stamp \"p.864\" but \"dice 664\" (orig. pagination error). Orig. p.864 [664].

author: Mariano G. Vallejo
summary: $102 supplied to the SF artillery piquete this month. stamp \"867\" / \"dice 667.\" Orig. p.867 [667].

author: José Mª Mederos
summary: SD artillery: iron 3 good / 5 bad / 1 useless; bronze 4 + 1 + 1 + 1, with pertrechos. stamp \"874–7\" / \"dice 674–7.\" Orig. pp.874–877 [674–7].

author: José Mª de Echeandía
themes: solis-revolt; deportation; san-blas; john-coffin-jones; herrera; solís; mexican-period
summary: Deportation roster of the Solís-revolt prisoners shipped on the American barque Volunteer to San Blas \"á la disposición del Supremo Gob.no\" — 15 individuals: José Mª Herrera (sub-comisario provisional, lodged \"en un camarote de proa de la cámara y recluido á él\"), Joaquín Solís (paisano — the revolt's leader), soldiers of the Monterey cav. Raimundo de la Torre, Pablo Vejar, Gonzalo & Victorino Altamirano, Leonardo Acedo; perm. artillery Máximo & Antonio Guerra, García Lara, cabos Mariano Vaquero & Andrés de León (shackled \"2 barras con un anillo cada uno\"); paisanos Melecio Soto, Sergio Escamilla; and presidiario Inés/José Polanco (10-yr sentence from 1827). Received by John Coffin Jones, master of the Volunteer. The core Solís-deportation record (cf. Docs 91, 131). Orig. pp.679–680.

author: Rodrigo del Pliego, Ayud. de la Capitanía del Puerto
summary: Arrival of the British brig Aquatic — 12 crew, 235 tons, from \"La Libertad,\" 162 days' sail, in ballast. Orig. p.691.

author: Domingo Carrillo
summary: Prisoners conducted on the national brig María Esther — 11 named: Pedro Sánchez, José Sacramento, Sebastián Núñez, Pascual Flores, Salomé Suárez, Francisco Vaca[?], Manuel Santillana, José Antonio López, José Ramón García, Manuel Mariano, Bernardo Pelíz[?]. Orig. p.692.

author: Agustín V. Zamorano
summary: List of those who abandoned Solís and presented themselves to the government (the revolt's defectors): Cabo Bernardo Curiel (5ª artillery), drummer Dionisio Correa, artillerymen [Joya?] Soto & J. Maldonado, Joaquín Felíz (apprehended, Monterey cabo), Juan Cordero (paisano), + others. Orig. pp.33–37.

author: Domingo Carrillo
summary: Ration tariff (tarifa de víveres) supplied weekly/monthly to the SB cavalry company's officers & troops — maize, beans, lard, salt, by married/single status, with costs. Orig. p.700.

author: Rodrigo del Pliego
summary: Monterey company 42 men (Alf. M. G. Vallejo attached). (n149 also lists Santiago Cárdenas, apprehended paisano — another Solís-era prisoner, pp.694–5.) Orig. pp.701–702.

author: Pliego
summary: Circular to the escort cabos from Soledad to San Luis Obispo: demand a passport of every traveler, detaining him until verified with his point of origin. (Internal passport control — Solís-revolt-aftermath security.) Orig. pp.730–731.

author: Pliego
summary: Order to hand over the escort to soldier Juan Ríos[?] and march; the company named him new habilitado. Orig. p.732.

author: Pliego
summary: Circular: Lic. Rafael Gómez took possession as Asesor del Territorio on the 18th (confirms \"Lic. Gómez\" = Rafael Gómez; cf. Docs 100/115/124). Orig. p.732.

author: Pliego
summary: Circular: Capt. (Lt. Col.) José María Padrés has resumed as Ayudante Inspector of California. (Padrés — the influential liberal officer behind the colonization/secularization plans.) Orig. p.732.

author: Pliego
summary: Escort circular re a habilitado appointment (apoderados Aceves & Juan Hernández). Orig. p.733.

author: Pliego
summary: Returns the causa vs. soldier Francisco Rubio[?] for modification on points indicated. Orig. pp.734–735.

author: (unsigned)
themes: supply; economy; hartnell; cooper; foreign-merchants; monterey; mexican-period
summary: Account of provisions/grain (frijol, maíz, trigo) received over the year 1827 for the Monterey presidio troops, from suppliers incl. Guillermo (William) Hartnell, Francisco Sánchez, Juan (John) Cooper, Misión del Carmelo, Misión de Santa Cruz — total $2,867. (Foreign-merchant supply of the presidio.) Orig. p.738.

author: Domingo Carrillo
summary: SB company 119 plazas; net pay due this month $1,167. Orig. pp.767–776.

author: Petronilo Ríos
summary: SF artillery census: bronze 6 serviceable + 1 useless; iron 5…; plaza carriages 3; marine carriages 8; arms, shot, powder. Orig. pp.779–783.

author: Various vecinos
summary: Petition that the full rigor of the law be applied to Vicente Sánchez for alleged misconduct in the elections to renew the ayuntamiento; Echeandía ordered the alcalde to investigate & report. Orig. pp.785–794.

author: Vicente Sánchez
summary: Three communications — to the alcalde (leave to tend his fields; a bondsman for his release) and two to the Jefe Político defending himself in the electoral dispute. Orig. pp.797–806.

author: Echeandía
themes: solis-revolt; trial; anti-spanish; luis-antonio-martínez; missionary; monterey
summary: Umbrella header for the Solís-revolt trial dossier: \"Pronunciamiento de Solís — Proceso contra los rebeldes — Indagaciones sobre la revolución por España — Sentencia contra Fr. Luis Antonio Martínez, Ministro de la Misión de San Luis Obispo.\" (The revolt's trial + the inquiry into a pro-Spanish dimension + the sentence against the SLO friar Luis Antonio Martínez. \"Véase á la vuelta.\") Orig. (intro to pp.152+).

author: Francisco Pacheco
summary: Report corroborating the imprisoned subalterns' statements (cabo Mariano Vaquero, artilleryman Antonio Guerra, paisanos Sergio Escamilla & Melitón Soto): Escamilla went as commissioner to SB and made the garrison revolt; names M.l Herrera as author of the Plan (per Solís's own declaration); Herrera induced soldier Pablo Vejar to rebel. Orig. (within the process).

author: Joaquín Solís, Mariano Vaquero, Andrés de León, Petronilo Ríos, J. de la Torre
themes: solis-revolt; plan; pronunciamiento; echeandía; diputación; foreigners; anti-spanish; monterey; 1829
summary: The Solís revolt's \"Plan de la revolución\" / \"Manifiesto al Público\" — a long indictment of Gov. Echeandía (corruption, self-enrichment, favoritism, suspending the Diputación, paralyzing agriculture/commerce/schools, the suspended Hacienda, impunity of crimes, troop \"hambre y desnudez\" and indiscipline) followed by 9 articles: (1) reconvene the Diputación; (2) Echeandía surrender all command to the Diputación, which names an interim chief until the new governor arrives; (3) ayuntamientos provisionally appoint Hacienda administrators; (4) discipline; (5–7) supply rules (missions requisitioned only via alcaldes w/ receipts); (8) property & persons respected, esp. \"los estrangeros que residen pacíficamente entre nosotros\" — with a Nota adicional extending the same protection to the Spaniards (suspending the expulsion → why the revolt was framed as \"por España\"). → verbatim in Expanded Entry D. Orig. pp.152–162.

author: Echeandía / fiscals Pacheco & Pliego
themes: solis-revolt; trial; sumaria; herrera; foreigners; hartnell; spence; stearns; missions; vaquero; ríos; solís; luis-antonio-martínez; vallejo; alvarado; josé-castro; monterey; 1829
summary: The Solís-revolt sumaria (declarations & confessions). Echeandía forwards declarations by Juan B. Alvarado, Ignacio Vallejo & José Castro, all naming J. Mª Herrera as the revolt's promoter / author of the Plan. Confessions of the accused (cabo Andrés de León, 21, owns his signature on the Plan but calls it Vaquero's work) narrate the night of the rising at Monterey: Solís named Mariano Vaquero sergeant/artillery commander; at 2 AM Vaquero woke the troops crying \"el enemigo está encima,\" and the rebels (Vaquero, Raimundo de la Torre, Pablo Vejar, Prudencio & Lucas Espinosa, Juan Berbón[?], Leonardo Aceves, José Rosas…) arrested the officers — Alf. M. G. Vallejo, Subtte. Juan José Rocha, Comisario Manuel Jimeno Casarín, sargto Andrés Cervantes — and jailed them; they also seized J. B. Alvarado & José Castro ( so the young Californios were here victims of the rebels, though suspected of anti-Echeandía agitation a year later — Doc 96); a party rode to San Carlos for the Alcalde (not found). Vaquero planned a forced 10% commercial contribution (~$1,000) to buy arms and flee by ship. Petronilo Ríos confessed he wrote the Plan from Herrera's dictation (15 Nov), read \"á Solís y á algunos extranjeros q[u]e allí estaban\" before the troops. Joaquín Solís's own confession (16 Nov; age 37, b. Huejotzingo, Puebla; a convict deported for public works) deflects authorship to Herrera, claiming he was deceived into thinking the document was a petition to the Comandante General, not a Plan; he took $1,006 of municipal funds. Named foreign-resident circle around Herrera (n173): \"los extranjeros q[u]e estaban con Herrera eran Guillermo (William) Hartnell, David Spence, Esteban (Stephen) Anderson, J. Mecala[?] (= McCulloch?), Abel Stearns y oficiales de buques balleneros.\" The missions gave the rebels forced \"voluntary\" contributions (San Luis Obispo, Purísima, Santa Inés; later Santa Cruz $100, San José $200, San Juan Bautista $40); friars José Sánchez (briefly named troop commander) and Vicente Sarría also figure (Sarría \"de indulto\" at Monterey). Foreigners & missions repeatedly placed at the revolt's core (cf. the Plan's Art. 8 + the \"revolución por España\" framing). A later confession denies knowing whether Solís had any conversation with Fr. Martínez \"sobre el pronunciamiento por España\" (the friar-conspiracy probe). Petronilo Ríos's story evolved — his Nov-1829 declaration (he wrote the Plan from Herrera's dictation) softened by Apr 1830 to merely signing it \"porque se le obligó\" (the draft in Vaquero's hand). The rank-and-file confessions (Espinosa, Rosas, Aces…) uniformly disclaim involvement and agree the revolt's origin was unpaid wages. Solís's formal confession before the consejo de guerra (21 Apr 1830, defender Tte. Manuel Ibarra) re-narrates the rising, names foreign merchant Juan (John) Cooper & \"[Simón] Bautista\" as witnesses, and claims he protected Alf. Vallejo when Arenas wanted to kill him \"como un perro\"; Solís surrendered under the April indulto to Alf. Francisco Pacheco. The remaining defendant confessions (Prudencio Espinosa, Pablo Vejar, Melitón Soto [the revolt's courier of the Plan to the ayuntamientos & diputados for $30], Máximo Guerra [a Chilean artilleryman, 31]…) repeat the arrests + unpaid-wages motive; a lista de presos names the 14 (Solís, R. de la Torre, Máximo Guerra, Melitón Soto, Vaquero, Antonio Guevara, Vejar, Ignacio Lara, Serapio Escamilla, Victorino & Gonzalo Altamirano, Leonardo Aces, Inés Solano, Gabriel de la Torre). The counter-revolution (n195): the Alcalde led armed foreigners gathered at John Cooper's house + Juan B. Alvarado + vecinos to retake the Monterey artillery barracks and arrest Vaquero & Cervantes — the foreign community as armed suppressors of the revolt (complicating the \"extranjeros con Herrera\" picture). Herrera's plot is said to have counted on Capt. Miguel González, a surgeon (Cobata[?]), and Diputado Vicente Sánchez (cf. Docs 143–144). retake scans n187 (p.183) & n193 (p.188); printed page = scan − 5 from n194. David Spence's deposition (comerciante, 23 June 1830) is the one clearly-deposed foreigner: Herrera summoned him, another ship captain & Stephen Anderson to his house, read them the Plan & asked their opinion (some answered \"está bueno\" out of courtesy; Solís present as signer) — concrete documented foreign involvement. The Hartnell/Anderson/Stearns/McCulloch circle was named in Solís's confession (n173), but only Spence's own deposition appears here (don't over-claim the others deposed). The proceso also appends Solís's actual revolt-period orders as evidence (n208) — incl. the 28 Nov salute order to Cabo Joaquín Piña (Mis.n Dolores): three artillery discharges (on entry, on the Plan's approval, on the troops' retirement) — the primary document behind Expanded Entry A. A deposition at n204 also explains Art. 8's pro-Spanish addition was inserted at the request of Spaniards fearing the proceedings. Scans n197–n208 gap-sampled (key findings captured, not every leaf transcribed — completeness pass pending). The \"revolución por España\" autos follow (Doc 149). Orig. pp.162–203+.

author: Echeandía / Zamorano (fiscal)
themes: solis-revolt; revolución-por-españa; fernando-vii; luis-antonio-martínez; missionary; expulsion; anderson; anti-spanish; máximo-guerra; santa-inés; monterey; 1830
summary: The \"revolución por España\" autos — the prosecution of Fr. Luis Antonio Martínez. Echeandía orders the inquiry; the file's cover sheet (carátula) reads \"Autos q[u]e aclaran q[u]e el objeto de la facción de Solís, era el de pronunciarse en favor del Gob.no Español.\" Testimony implicates the San Luis Obispo missionary Fr. Luis Antonio Martínez: Lázaro Piña (23 Jan) saw artilleryman Máximo Guerra (a Chilean) in league with Fr. Martínez at Santa Inés, who advised \"pronunciarse por España\"; a paper read \"Viva Fernando 7º\" (with 3 initials); the Padre promised to recommend them to \"su amo Fernandito\" (Ferdinand VII). Francisco Becerra (San Blas inf.): the friar [or Sarría?] at SLO offered Guerra money & goods to pronounce for Spain. Calls to prosecute Fr. Martínez, Máximo Guerra, R. de la Torre, Melitón Soto. Fr. Martínez allegedly had ~$6,000 embarked on the schooner La Paloma to flee to Spain (per Capt. Miguel González, n222); the planned cry \"dar el grito por España\" at SLO with 14 vaqueros per mission. Fr. Martínez's own defense (n228): he denies the pro-Spanish charge, claiming he was an early supporter of independence (\"quizás fui yo el primero de mis hermanos… q[u]e dije, tal debe seguir la suerte de la república; así opinaba y así opino ahora\"), requests his passport to leave the Republic, protests the violation of civil & canon law, and cites his gota (gout). The asesor's deliberation (n234): given his age/ailments and the fear that the other missionaries would abandon the missions if a \"persona consagrada\" were jailed, he is not imprisoned but expelled — the record finding he \"auxilió á la facción de Solís p[ar]a el pronunciamiento en favor de la España.\" The verdict (n235–n236): a war-council of officers voted 5 to 1 — citing the territory's \"tranquilidad y seguridad\" and Martínez's \"inculpabilidad\" (i.e., not a guilty finding) plus his 26 Dec 1826 declaration that he did not conform with \"nuestro actual sistema\" (the republic) — to compel his expulsion on any non-Spanish ship (with a bond to carry him to Europe or the US). Frs. Pedro & Juan Cabot filed manifestos in his favor; fiscal Tte. de Ing. Miguel García Lobato. Fr. Martínez was put aboard the foreign merchant Stephen Anderson's English frigate \"Thomas Nowlan\" (7 Mar 1832), swearing not to disembark at Manila or the Sandwich Islands [Hawaii] but to take the first ship to Europe. ( Anderson, named in the Solís depositions, owned the deportation ship — the foreign community central yet again.) This closes the Solís dossier and Tomo LXXII (\"Fin del tomo 72,\" n236). n209–n234 gap-sampled (completeness pass pending for verbatim depositions). Orig. pp.204–231 (stamp \"659\" = 359).

author: Agustín V. Zamorano, Comand.te Gral (fiscal Juan M. Ibarra)
summary: Causa criminal vs. cavalry soldier Juan Antonio Arces for treacherously wounding the Santa Bárbara neófito Francisco Javier (12 Aug 1831). Asesor Lic. Rafael Gómez (7 Sep 1832): the wound was not grave (the victim recovered quickly, \"fué por causa de sus embriagueces\") and had no aggravating circumstance — Arces had already purged the offense with ~6 months' prison; released to continue service. Orig. pp.21–106.

author: Lic. Rafael Gómez, Asesor (fiscal Rodrigo del Pliego)
themes: indigenous-justice; gentiles; horse-raiding; tulares; soledad; legal-status; gómez; mexican-period
summary: Robo de caballos by Tulares gentiles. The gentile Chaguanic[?] (ranchería ~20 leagues from San Miguel) + 3 others stole 102 horses (he confessed 111) from Mission Soledad (Mar 1831); pursued by mission people — one accomplice (Tancho) killed resisting, 2 escaped, Chaguanic captured (confession via interpreter). Fiscal recommended 4 yrs presidio. Asesor Gómez: a gentile \"no está sujeto á las leyes penales nuestras\" (though force, even lethal, may repel his aggressions); recommends he be freed & placed in a mission (Monterey–SF) for instruction \"en las verdades del Evangelio… [y] lo paternal del Gob.no,\" so neither impunity nor offense to \"los derechos de la humanidad.\" Note: \"Fugó antes de q[u]e se pusiese en libertad.\" Orig. pp.108–129.

author: Gov. Manuel Victoria (fiscal José Sánchez)
themes: indigenous-justice; neophyte; theft; missions-critique; secularization; missionary-abuse; vallejo; gómez; victoria; monterey; mexican-period
summary: Causa: Pedro, a San Diego neophyte (then a presidiario at SF), stole objects from Sra. [Josefa?] Villela's house (15 June); apprehended by Alf. M. G. Vallejo; fiscal José Sánchez asked 6 mo public works. Asesor Gómez rebukes the fiscal for basing the penalty not on any cited law but on \"una larga lamentación de la ignorancia de los indios\" and on blaming the Missionaries for the punishments inflicted on Indians \"á vista ó tolerancia de las autoridades\"; Gómez sides with the defender, recommends 3 yrs presidio (concurrent w/ the SF term) + a \"severa reprensión\"; Gov. Victoria confirmed (2 Oct). The dossier quotes fiscal José Sánchez's extraordinary anti-mission diatribe in full — the missions \"el atrocísimo sistema de misiones por la fuerza,\" neophytes \"cuasi-esclavos,\" flogging \"el castigo favorito de los misioneros,\" a \"sistema despótico contra todas las leyes de un país libre\" — a raw secularization-era critique (quoted disapprovingly by Gómez). → Expanded Entry E. Orig. pp.182–235.

author: Rodrigo del Pliego, Juez fiscal
themes: military-justice; capital-case; execution; robbery; missions; san-carlos; gómez; monterey; mexican-period
summary: Robbery of the Mission San Carlos almacén (storehouse; reported 13 May 1831 by Padre Fr. Ramón Abella to Gov. Victoria). Pliego investigated the tendajos for the stolen goods; culprits: presidiario [Simón/José] Aguilar, the civilian Eduardo Lagarra[?], and Andrés (San Carlos neophyte); a neófito Rafael held first (a piece of cotín on him). The fiscal sought the death penalty (\"pasados por las armas\") for Aguilar & Lagarra; the 13-yr-old accomplice → 200 lashes. Asesor Gómez: Aguilar merits \"el último suplicio\" as a \"ladrón famoso\" (prior robbery conviction). Sentence: Aguilar & Lagarra \"pasados por las armas\" (SHOT) — executed 28 May 1831 in the fiscal's presence; the neophyte Andrés (who held the almacén key) made to watch, then 100 lashes + a corma + 6 months' forced mission labor; 4 other Indians freed with a warning. Gov. Victoria confirmed (26 May). Orig. pp.287–357.

author: Rodrigo del Pliego, Comand.te y habilitado
summary: Monterey presidial company Aug 1831: Pliego (alf./cmdt.); 3 sargtos, 1 cabo, 25 soldados — of these 5 imprisoned at San Blas (the Solís deportees) — total 30 hombres; haberes en activo $589. Orig. pp.358–366.

author: Capt. Pablo de la Portilla
themes: foreign-ship; american; deserters; otter-hunting; northwest-coast-indians; oahu; san-diego; maritime; mexican-period
summary: \"Tres extranjeros llegaron á la frontera\" — interrogation of three foreign deserters (per Gov. Victoria's 22 Aug order to identify their ship). James Dawson deposed: they deserted from the American otter-hunting brig Grafton (from the NW Coast; 24 crew + 33 Northwest-Coast Indian hunters; armed with 18 + 8 cannon + escopetas; took ~150 otters in 6 days on this coast; touched at San Quintín; the captain meant to hunt ~3 more months, then sail to Oahu). The captain had been at Monterey 2 yrs before on the \"Volunteer\" trading horses. Deserted \"por mal trato.\" Orig. pp.367–372.

author: Gov. Manuel Victoria
themes: politics; san-josé; monterey; ayuntamiento; diputación; federalism; anti-victoria; subversion; duarte; buelna; josé-castro; alvarado; peralta; 1831
summary: Causa criminal vs. José Mariano Duarte, Alcalde of San José, for getting the ayuntamiento to sign \"subversive\" papers. Denounced by síndico Luis Chabolla[?]; the drafts (in the archive, extracted by Regidor Flores) deemed \"subversivos y de mucha malicia.\" Doc nº1 (in the Alcalde's own hand): a federalist manifesto — \"este Ayuntam.to ha llegado á conocer sus derechos y el alcance de sus fuerzas\" — calling (per constitutional art. 27) for the Exma Diputación to be convened at Monterey at the ayuntamientos' petition. Doc nº2: accusations the alcalde was caught in \"tratos ilícitos\" with Ignacia Padilla at night (regidores Joaquín Higuera + Fernando Félix expelling him). The political agitation echoes the Solís Plan's central demand (reconvene the Diputación) and is an apparent precursor to the Nov-1831 anti-Victoria revolt. Docs nº3–4: the acta (1 June 1831) expanding the San José ayuntamiento — adding 2 regidores (Fernando Félix & Cnt. Rodríguez), secretary José Reyes Berreyesa, depositario the retired sgt Luis Peralta (the prominent ranchero). Witness Luis Chabolla (síndico): Duarte's circular invited the Territory's ayuntamientos to press the Cmdt. Gen. to convene the Diputación Provincial (\"redunda en la felicidad del país\"); Chabolla demurred (the matter belonged to the Diputación itself). Also alleged arbitrary acts (jailing a woman & asking Santa Clara's padre to shackle/shear her — the padre refused) + a municipal-funds dispute. Escribano Petronilo Ríos. Secretary José Reyes Berreyesa refused to copy/sign paper nº1, warning it meant attacking the Jefe Superior (Gov. Victoria); the Alcalde nonetheless sent the copy to Antonino Buelna (alcalde at Monterey — a 1831-revolt figure). Witness Mª Ignacia Padilla (true name Mª Presentación Lóngora[?]) detailed the alcalde's mistreatment (shackling her in jail). Duarte's confession (n264–66): the plan originated at Monterey on 7 Apr 1831 in conversation with Antonino Buelna (Monterey alcalde), the Secretary of the Provincial Diputación [= J. B. Alvarado], and José Castro — to petition the Cmdt. Gen. to convene the Diputación; Buelna later came to San José, and Duarte signed the document Buelna forwarded to Monterey. (So the San José case ties directly into the Buelna / Castro / Alvarado federalist circle behind the Nov-1831 anti-Victoria revolt — the same young Californios as the Solís pasquines, Doc 96.) Fiscal Pliego: Duarte guilty by his own confession + the witnesses; the case moved to Monterey to depose the implicated Antonino Buelna (19 Aug), who denied remembering the Diputación plan. Defender Tte. Juan Mª Ibarra. José Castro (Monterey ayuntamiento secretary) & J. B. Alvarado (Diputación secretary) both deposed — distancing themselves from a direct alcalde-to-alcalde conspiracy while confirming the Monterey ayuntamiento DID form actas to convene the Diputación. Gov. Victoria's framing (24 Aug oficio): the agitation was \"procurar por medios ilegales se reuniese la Diputación, con fines siniestros y muy contrarios á las determinaciones del Sup.o Gob.,\" with Buelna the key inducer — the government casting the federalist movement as subversion (the tension that exploded into the Nov-1831 revolt). The fiscal's summary (n280–85) restates the 3 charges: (1) pressing the ayuntamientos to convene the Diputación (a paper in Duarte's own hand, f.2); (2) his 7 Jul suspension over the night-time illicit-relations incident (f.3); (3) the arbitrary 1 June election of 2 regidores + secretary + depositario (f.5). n275–n285 gap-sampled (witness ratifications/careos + the summary; payload captured — completeness pass could fill these leaves). Conclusion (n286–88): the Asesor ruled the military lacked jurisdiction over alcaldes acting as first-instance judges, so the case was remitted to the Mexican Supreme Court (ministers Flores/Navarrete/Guzmán, Feb–Mar 1832) for civil handling + a Jefe-Político report on the alcaldes' \"crime\" of trying to convene the Diputación. By then Victoria had already been overthrown — the prosecution was moot.

author: José Antonio Facio, Secret.o de Guerra y Marina
summary: \"Sobre supresión de empleos\" — asks the Cmdt. Gen. to justify more fully the proposed measures, esp. suppressing the Ayudante Inspector & related posts deemed superfluous in the reglamento. Orig. p.490.

author: Echeandía
summary: \"Sobre cambio en misiones\" — secularization: per the 13 Sep 1813 law, the missions' temporal administration to change; asks the Bishop to resolve what is best for the \"nuevos pueblos\" spiritually — convert them into curatos with canonically-provided párrocos, or leave them to the Religiosos. (The 6 Jan 1831 secularization measure addressed to the Bishop — parallel to Doc 69.) Orig. p.494.

author: Echeandía
summary: Correspondence cluster (Con. 6–9): notice of Manuel Jimeno Casarín's resignation as Contador of the Monterey aduana (29 Nov) & Antonio Mª Osio's entry; suppression of the Monterey resguardo command; Jimeno's refusal of the Contador appointment (issued by the Méx. Hacienda 30 Sep 1829). Orig. pp.495–500.

author: Andrés Cervantes, Sargto 2º
summary: SD artillery detachment (Cervantes + ~9–12 soldados; sargto Mederos + 1 passed to SB); reviewed before Juan Bandini. Orig. pp.510–517.

author: Cabo Lázaro Piña
summary: SF artillery detachment: 7 soldados under Piña from June (earlier cabos Ignacio Soto, then Petronilo Ríos); reviewed before Antonio Mª Osio (and earlier M. G. Vallejo). Orig. pp.509–523.

author: Sargto José Mª Mederos
summary: SB artillery detachment: 1 sargto, 1 cabo, 6 soldados = 8; reviewed before Anastasio Carrillo (postmaster). Orig. pp.513–531.

author: Domingo Carrillo, Comand.te
summary: SB presidial company 1831: Capt. José de la Guerra (absent); Tte. Maitorena (absent in México); 46 present + invalids (3 sargtos/1 cabo/18 sold.); haberes $7,756 + invalids $1,283; the company doing escoltas at missions SLO, Purísima, Sta Inés, SB, San Buenaventura, San Fernando. Orig. pp.555–643.

author: Ignacio Martínez, Comand.te
summary: SF presidial company 1831: Tte. Martínez; Alf. M. G. Vallejo; sargto grad. de alf. José Sánchez; 30 present (varied 35–44); pay declining $1,047→$731 across the year; reviewed before Antonio Mª Osio. Orig. pp.644–664.

author: Ignacio Martínez, Habilitado
summary: Merchandise price list: paño azul ord.o 22 reales/vara, paño de grana $3, botones $4/gruesa, mascadas negras, hilo, manta blanca 3¼/vara, jeans, pañitos — prices of imported goods at SF. Orig. p.666.

author: J. M. Ibarra
themes: maritime; foreign-ship; commerce; sitka; russian-california; monterey; 1831
summary: Register of ship arrivals & departures at Monterey, 28 Dec 1830 – 31 Aug 1831: Entradas incl. barca Am. Volunteer (Capt. Carter), berg. Am. Convoy (from the Sandwich Isl.), frag. Am. Pocahontas, frag. Louisa (from Sitka — RAC), balandra Guillermo Chiquito, berg. ingl. Ayacucho (from Callao), berg. Mex. Catalina (Capt. Holmes, from San Blas); + the matching Salidas. Foreign maritime traffic at Monterey, incl. the Russian Sitka connection. Orig. pp.668–669.

author: [José Antonio] Tapia
summary: \"Sedición de Solís\" — Tapia petitions to be exempted from prison, noting he was forced to come to SB with 30 men \"para sostener al Gob.no legítimo, y contra la revolución de Solís\" (a loyalist who fought Solís seeking relief). Orig. pp.676–677.

author: Domingo Carrillo, Comand.te interino
summary: \"Sedición de Solís\" — the SB guard's defense against Solís (8 soldiers, 1 artillery sargto + 6 men, 1 cannon) under Echeandía; Manuel Cota arrived with 30 men from Los Ángeles (incl. José Antonio Tapia) to reinforce SB by Echeandía's order; served until Solís didn't come, then returned to LA. Orig. pp.678–679.

author: Tiburcio Tapia, alcalde de LA (1830)
summary: Certifies Echeandía ordered him (Jan 1830) to mobilize 30 Los Ángeles men under José Mª Cota to help defend SB against Solís; José Antonio Tapia among them (in LA under bond). (The \"LA citizens mobilized\" material — the loyalist side of the Solís revolt.) Orig. p.680.

author: Manuel Crespo, sangrador de la Comp.a presidial
summary: Brief record — Crespo as bloodletter/surgeon of the presidial company. Orig. p.698.

author: Agustín V. Zamorano, Juez fiscal (sec. Antonio del Valle)
themes: fiscal-corruption; malversation; herrera; solis-revolt-backstory; nile; tepic; intendant-ordinance; zamorano; bandini; monterey; 1827
summary: Secret causa vs. subcomisario José Mª Herrera for malversación de caudales públicos. Echeandía commissioned Zamorano (25 Apr 1827, SB) to secretly investigate Herrera's \"dispersión\" of public funds during his management. Witness Capt. José de la Guerra: Herrera received $22,000 cash for 7 months' troop pay, but only ~$3,600/month reached the SB company — implying diversion. (The pre-Solís backstory: Herrera under embezzlement investigation in 1827, before becoming the 1829 revolt's financial mastermind — cf. the Plan's grievance over his suspension.) Filed out of order in this 1831 tomo. Witnesses (de la Guerra, Maitorena, Carlos Antonio Carrillo, Juan Bandini, Romualdo Pacheco) establish the $22,000 was shipped from Acapulco on the berg. Morelos then via San Blas on the goleta Nieves (toward Loreto); the shortfall traced to Herrera spending part on goods (cigars, effects) at Tepic; only ~$3,600/mo reached the SB company. Also names a customs/otter-duty waiver Herrera granted the American brig Nile (Oct 1825). Merchant Luis Bringas (the goods' seller) deposed at LA: the goods (~$45,000 worth) came from Tepic (financed by Saltillo merchant Salvador Tejada + a Herrera relative, guarantor Ramón Pareja); Herrera helped ship them on the warship Morelos, and they were sold from Herrera's own house then peddled at the presidios/pueblos/missions — the heart of the malversation question (public office aiding a private import venture). Courier Ignacio Mª Alvarado (paid $30 by Bringas to carry letters to Herrera) deposed; financial detail names a draft to the American merchant Guillermo (William A.) Gale. Conclusion (n312, fiscal Capt. Pablo de la Portilla, LA 30 June 1827): Bringas — and by extension Herrera — found complicit \"en el fraude de los intereses nacionales.\" Fiscal Zamorano's overall conclusion (Monterey, 6 July 1827, n313–16): no doubt Herrera spent a considerable part of the $22,000 on goods at Tepic — only $6,500–$8,500 entered the territory as cash, leaving a ~$13,000 shortfall (≈ the value of the 20+ extra fardos landed at Monterey); Herrera infringed the ordenanza de Intendentes barring a comisario from any commerce, esp. with public funds; recommends a letrado's opinion + the Diputación member Juan Bandini's deposition. Process handed to the Cmdt. Gen. 5 July 1827. Orig. pp.701–760.

author: Mariano G. Vallejo, escribano de la causa
themes: fiscal-corruption; forgery; herrera; bringas; vallejo; monterey; 1830
summary: A second Herrera causa (1830): the subcomisario José Mª Herrera accused of falsifying signatures to fake documents he sent to Luis Bringas. (Savage: \"de esta causa se hicieron estos actos minuciosos en otro proceso.\") A forgery charge atop the embezzlement (Doc 171) — the cumulative case against the Solís-revolt subcomisario. Orig. pp.762–777.

author: Lic. Rafael Gómez, Asesor (fiscal sargto Dámaso Antonio Rodríguez)
themes: indigenous-protest; missions-critique; mission-decline; asset-stripping; secularization; santa-barbara; gómez; neophytes; mexican-period
summary: Causa vs. Juan Pablo, a Santa Bárbara neófito, for inducing ~10 fellow neophytes to make a \"representación violenta\" against their Padre Ministro — complaining the Padre had sold / was selling the mission's cattle, the roof-tiles of the mission houses, the mules, and the beach house (asset-stripping; recruited via Francisco Soya[?], Hilario the blacksmith, et al.). They petitioned alférez Domingo Carrillo. Fiscal sought 1 yr public works. Asesor Gómez sides with the neophytes: free Juan Pablo, and instead open an investigation into the Padre, who — if the complaint proves true — is to be told \"no estar facultado p[ar]a vender las fincas de la misión, y mucho menos p[ar]a acabar los alimentos y vestuario á los neófitos\" when there are esquilmos to cover it. (End not present.) A striking mission-asset-stripping / indigenous-protest case on the eve of secularization (pairs with Expanded Entry E). Orig. pp.779–800.

author: fiscal José Sánchez (earlier José Berreyesa)
themes: indigenous-justice; neophyte-homicide; wrongful-imprisonment; fiscal-falsification; santa-clara; vallejo; gómez; figueroa; san-francisco; mexican-period
summary: Causa vs. Luis, a Santa Clara neófito, for killing Atanasio (another SC neophyte). A long case (1830–1833, ~114 pp. of declarations; several Indians — León, Marcos, Lucas — held on suspicion); fiscals Berreyesa then Sánchez; defender Alf. M. G. Vallejo. Luis petitioned Gov. Figueroa (arrived 1833) over ~2 years' imprisonment for a calumny. Asesor Gómez (13 Oct 1831): the evidence is weak — absolve Luis; and open a sumaria against fiscal sargto Berreyesa for falsifying the witness declarations (Vicente & Tiliburo[?]). The case stalled during the 1831–32 political turmoil (Victoria's overthrow). Gov. Figueroa (16 Feb 1833) freed Luis, restored to his mission after ~2.5 years' wrongful imprisonment. A miscarriage-of-justice / fiscal-falsification case spanning the Victoria→Figueroa transition. Orig. pp.2–108, 116–127.

author: Agustín V. Zamorano, Comand.te
summary: Monterey presidial company muster, May 1834: Capt. Zamorano; Tte. Gervasio Argüello (absent in Guadalajara); 1º Alf. José Sánchez; 2º Alf. Ignacio del Valle; 2 sargtos, 1 clarín, 4 cabos, 23 soldados = 34 hombres; reviewed before Sub-comisario José Rafael González + Lt. Col. Nicolás Gutiérrez. Orig. pp.112–113.

author: Agustín V. Zamorano, Secret.o de la Comand.a Gen.
summary: Causa: a representation by Capt. Miguel González against José Joaquín Maitorena — never prosecuted (no officers available under Victoria, 1831); the Méx. Superioridad referred it to Gov. Figueroa (Apr 1833); Zamorano certifies (16 Jan 1834) the motive ceased on Maitorena's death, and Figueroa ordered it suspended & archived. Orig. pp.128–129.

author: Juan Mª Ibarra, Fiscal
themes: capital-case; execution; robbery; treasury; atanasio; neophyte; monterey; gómez; victoria; 1831
summary: Causa for the robbery of the Monterey national-treasury almacén (reported 28 Sep 1830 by comisario Manuel Jimeno to Alcalde Mariano Soberanes), with the Indian Atanasio as the accused. (Header: \"Ejecución del reo\" — i.e., the accused was executed.) Fiscal Ibarra (16 Mar 1831) asked 2 yrs presidio (per the 1788 law on presidiarios robbing the Hacienda; goods worth $219); defender Vicente Ríos pleaded Atanasio's minority (<18) & that the value didn't reach $100; but Asesor Gómez found the crime aggravated (against national interests, in an official's house, by his own domestic). Outcome: Atanasio condemned to death and SHOT on 26 April 1831 at 11 AM — Gómez applied the military ordinance (R.O. 31 Aug 1772) + old Castilian law, \"pasado por las armas\" (the gallows having been abolished by the 1812–13 Cortes & 1832 Mexican decrees); Victoria confirmed 23 Apr, capilla then execution, fiscal & escribano certifying. A harsh capital outcome — execution for a ~$219 treasury robbery by a minor neophyte. → Expanded Entry F (special record). Orig. pp.180–309.

author: José Fernández del Campo, Alf. y comand.te principal de artillería
summary: Brief artillery muster/note. Orig. p.445.

author: Juan José Rocha, Comand.te Mil.
themes: incest; estupro; gender; sexual-violence; san-diego; military-justice; mexican-period
summary: Causa \"por estupro é incesto\": the Sub-Tte. (de premio, artillery) Aniceto Zabaleta accused by his daughter María Dolores Zabaleta of taking her virginity and impregnating her, then beating her and trying to blame the pregnancy on Santiago Linares (and an alleged liaison with Ramón Sotelo, which Sotelo denies). Escribano cabo José Morales. Zabaleta's confession denies the incest, claiming he was investigating his daughter's relationships with Vicente Elizalde and Ramón Sotelo and trying to find the father of her pregnancy \"para cubrir su honor\"; Dolores maintains her father raped & impregnated her. A he-said/she-said domestic sumaria (the Leyva family also involved). Midwives María Ignacia López & Luz Ruiz found Dolores pregnant with no marks of violence. Asesor Gómez's resolution: insufficient evidence to convict — absolve Zabaleta provisionally (re-prosecutable), and place Dolores \"en depósito\" (protective custody) away from her father. No final decree from Victoria appears, \"probablemente debido á los disturbios políticos\" (the Nov-1831 revolt). n337–n345 gap-sampled. sensitive content — transcribed as a documented criminal record.

author: Sargto José Mª Mederos[?]
summary: Monterey artillery detachment Dec 1832: 2 sargtos, 1 tambor, 8 cabos, 32 artilleros; haberes & gratif. $752. Orig. pp.514–515.

author: Juan Antonio Muñoz
summary: Monterey artillery detachment 1833: 1 sargto 1º, 2 sargtos 2º, 1 tambor, 8 cabos, 28 soldados = 42 hombres. Orig. pp.516–521.

author: Anastasio Carrillo, Habil.do
summary: SB presidial company Nov 1832: Cap. José de la Guerra; Tte. Maitorena; 1º Alf. Domingo Carrillo; 2º Alf. Anastasio Carrillo; 2 cabos, 33 soldados = 39 hombres (+ 19 invalids); haberes ~$1,890. Orig. pp.535–564.

author: Agustín V. Zamorano, Comand.te accidental
themes: ávila-sedition; 1832-civil-war; vicente-sánchez; immunity; amnesty; convicts; presidiarios; zamorano; figueroa; post-victoria; santa-bárbara; monterey; 1832
summary: \"Sedición de Antonio Ávila\" — investigation of a convict-band uprising in the 1832 civil conflict. Antonio Ávila led a band of presidiarios (from Santa Margarita) recruited & armed by Don Vicente Sánchez (the LA diputado; arms taken from Santa Bárbara, distributed at the Cerrito de los Voluntarios). The plan: join the Mazatlán squadron under Tte. Ibarra to fight the \"San Diego party\" / oppose Zamorano (the northern Comandante), framed \"en defensa del Supremo Gob.no\"; comisario Joaquín Gómez (SB) supplied goods; documents from Mariano Estrada, Rafael Gómez, Joaquín Gómez named. Six convict-deserters (Ignacio Olgadillo, Antonio Rivera, José Mª Blanquel, Catarino López, Joaquín Alfaro, Guadalupe Martínez) deposed — they deserted on realizing they'd been deceived by Sánchez & Ávila. A key datapoint on the 1832 post-Victoria civil war (Zamorano's north vs. Echeandía/Sánchez's south). Vicente Sánchez claimed parliamentary immunity (as a territorial Diputado, Const. Art. 336 + the 24 May 1813 Cortes decree) to refuse testifying; Asesor Gómez ruled the immunity didn't cover an act \"muy distante de sus atribuciones.\" Conclusion: Gov. Figueroa (1833) dismissed the whole case under the AMNESTY law of 25 April 1832 — the accused already free & the cause unsubstantiated; sobreseído y archivado. n355–n362 gap-sampled (repetitive convict-deserter testimony).

author: Gov. Figueroa
summary: Routine causa vs. the presidiario Francisco Badelli[?] for robbery (some botes de cebo); reassigned after the prior fiscal (Dámaso Antonio Rodríguez) didn't follow the formulary. Savage: \"la causa no ofrece interés.\" Orig. pp.608–725.

author: Anastasio Carrillo
